title: vi-mode plugin cursor won't set correctly for visual mode option
body:
### Describe the bug

setting solid block =2  for the visual setting doesn't work but the others do:

these are my settings

# Control whether to force a redraw on each mode change.
#
# Resetting the prompt on every mode change can cause lag when switching modes.
# This is especially true if the prompt does things like checking git status.
#
# Set to "true" to force the prompt to reset on each mode change.
# Unset or set to any other value to do the opposite.
#
# The default is not to reset, unless we're showing the mode in RPS1.
typeset -g VI_MODE_RESET_PROMPT_ON_MODE_CHANGE
# Control whether to change the cursor style on mode change.
#
# Set to "true" to change the cursor on each mode change.
# Unset or set to any other value to do the opposite.
typeset -g VI_MODE_SET_CURSOR=true

# Control how the cursor appears in the various vim modes. This only applies
# if $VI_MODE_SET_CURSOR=true.
#
# See https://vt100.net/docs/vt510-rm/DECSCUSR for cursor styles
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_NORMAL=1
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_VISUAL=2
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT=5
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_OPPEND=0

typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=main

function _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap() {
  [[ "$VI_MODE_SET_CURSOR" = true ]] || return

  # https://vt100.net/docs/vt510-rm/DECSCUSR
  local _shape=0
  case "${1:-${VI_KEYMAP:-main}}" in
    main)    _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT ;; # vi insert: line
    viins)   _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT ;; # vi insert: line
    isearch) _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT ;; # inc search: line
    command) _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT ;; # read a command name
    vicmd)   _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_NORMAL ;; # vi cmd: block
    visual)  _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_VISUAL ;; # vi visual mode: block
    viopp)   _shape=$VI_MODE_CURSOR_OPPEND ;; # vi operation pending: blinking block
    *)       _shape=0 ;;
  esac
  printf $'\e[%d q' "${_shape}"
}

# Updates editor information when the keymap changes.
function zle-keymap-select() {
  # update keymap variable for the prompt
  typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=$KEYMAP

  if [[ "${VI_MODE_RESET_PROMPT_ON_MODE_CHANGE:-}" = true ]]; then
    zle reset-prompt
    zle -R
  fi
  _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap "${VI_KEYMAP}"
}
zle -N zle-keymap-select

# These "echoti" statements were originally set in lib/key-bindings.zsh
# Not sure the best way to extend without overriding.
function zle-line-init() {
  local prev_vi_keymap
  prev_vi_keymap="${VI_KEYMAP:-}"
  typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=main
  [[ "$prev_vi_keymap" != 'main' ]] && [[ "${VI_MODE_RESET_PROMPT_ON_MODE_CHANGE:-}" = true ]] && zle reset-prompt
  (( ! ${+terminfo[smkx]} )) || echoti smkx
  _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap "${VI_KEYMAP}"
}
zle -N zle-line-init

function zle-line-finish() {
  typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=main
  (( ! ${+terminfo[rmkx]} )) || echoti rmkx
  _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap default
}
zle -N zle-line-finish

bindkey -v
bindkey -M viins jj vi-cmd-mode 

# allow vv to edit the command line (standard behaviour)
# autoload -Uz edit-command-line
# zle -N edit-command-line
# bindkey -M vicmd 'vv' edit-command-line

# allow ctrl-p, ctrl-n for navigate history (standard behaviour)
bindkey '^P' up-history
bindkey '^N' down-history

# allow ctrl-h, ctrl-w, ctrl-? for char and word deletion (standard behaviour)
bindkey '^?' backward-delete-char
bindkey '^h' backward-delete-char
bindkey '^w' backward-kill-word

# allow ctrl-r and ctrl-s to search the history
bindkey '^r' history-incremental-search-backward
bindkey '^s' history-incremental-search-forward

# allow ctrl-a and ctrl-e to move to beginning/end of line
bindkey '^a' beginning-of-line
bindkey '^e' end-of-line

function wrap_clipboard_widgets() {
  # NB: Assume we are the first wrapper and that we only wrap native widgets
  # See zsh-autosuggestions.zsh for a more generic and more robust wrapper
  local verb="$1"
  shift

  local widget
  local wrapped_name
  for widget in "$@"; do
    wrapped_name="_zsh-vi-${verb}-${widget}"
    if [ "${verb}" = copy ]; then
      eval "
        function ${wrapped_name}() {
          zle .${widget}
          printf %s \"\${CUTBUFFER}\" | clipcopy 2>/dev/null || true
        }
      "
    else
      eval "
        function ${wrapped_name}() {
          CUTBUFFER=\"\$(clippaste 2>/dev/null || echo \$CUTBUFFER)\"
          zle .${widget}
        }
      "
    fi
    zle -N "${widget}" "${wrapped_name}"
  done
}

wrap_clipboard_widgets copy vi-yank vi-yank-eol vi-backward-kill-word vi-change-whole-line vi-delete vi-delete-char
wrap_clipboard_widgets paste vi-put-{before,after}
unfunction wrap_clipboard_widgets

# if mode indicator wasn't setup by theme, define default, we'll leave INSERT_MODE_INDICATOR empty by default
if [[ -z "$MODE_INDICATOR" ]]; then
  MODE_INDICATOR='%B%F{red}<%b<<%f'
fi

function vi_mode_prompt_info() {
  # If we're using the prompt to display mode info, and we haven't explicitly
  # disabled "reset prompt on mode change", then set it here.
  #
  # We do that here instead of the `if` statement below because the user may
  # set RPS1/RPROMPT to something else in their custom config.
  : "${VI_MODE_RESET_PROMPT_ON_MODE_CHANGE:=true}"

  echo "${${VI_KEYMAP/vicmd/$MODE_INDICATOR}/(main|viins)/$INSERT_MODE_INDICATOR}"
}

# define right prompt, if it wasn't defined by a theme
if [[ -z "$RPS1" && -z "$RPROMPT" ]]; then
  RPS1='$(vi_mode_prompt_info)'
fi


### Steps to reproduce

# See https://vt100.net/docs/vt510-rm/DECSCUSR for cursor styles
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_NORMAL=1
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_VISUAL=2
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_INSERT=5
typeset -g VI_MODE_CURSOR_OPPEND=0

solid block cursor in visual mode in iterm2

title: Terminal freeze or not responding when pressing tab in a scp command
body:
### Describe the bug

Hi, I am currently on mac using oh-my-zsh in iTerm2 with only 2 plugins 
oh-my-zsh will freeze or cause iterm2 not to respond when trying to tab in a scp command for example, when typing the following and then press tab, it will freeze.
	-
	  ```
	  scp abc@xyz:/some/random/path/here
	  ```
	- I did some tests with commenting out `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh`. it is no longer there. Commenting out plugins like syntax-highlighting and auto-suggestions, it is still there.
	- Even different hostnames/addresses will result in different extents of delay: some are okay, but slightly slow, and some will be frozen for a longer time.
	- You can type without any delay, but as soon as you press tab and try to type, it will delay and become very slow
	-  I tested on both my mac with oh-my-zsh on, and it will reproduce.

### Steps to reproduce

- Type in the command line (don't press enter, and abc@xyz should be a real address, or it will not reproduce)

  scp abc@xyz:/some/random/path/here

iTerm2 will freeze and become not responding.

title: Tab completion for alias commands under mac osx not work
body:
### Describe the bug

If some command is set as an alias, tab completion no longer works for command arguments.

### Steps to reproduce

Let's take the 'cd' command for example.

1. In order to list all files in the new folder after 'cd' automatically, set the 'cd' command as the following alias, in .zshrc file:
`_my_cd() { IFS=$'\n'; cd $1; ls -lah; };`
`alias cd='_my_cd'`

2. Source ~/.zshrc or open new terminal window.

3. Press 'cd' + 'space' + 'tab', expecting candidate folders to be completed, while no completion is made.
`cd `

### Expected behavior

Auto completion works.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

macOS Ventura 13.3

### Zsh version

5.9

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

1. If 'cd' is not set as an alias, everything works fine.
2. Under Ubuntu 20.04.5, everything works fine even when 'cd' is set as an alias.
3. For OTHER alias commands, the same bug ALSO EXISTS.

This bug seems to be introduced by recent updates, for the mentioned alias has been working perfectly for years under osx as well as zsh, until latest oh-my-zsh update.

author: GMudide

title: is_update_available function slows startup a lot
body: ### Describe the bug I have been noticing degraded startup performance recently so I used the profiler to see what might be causing it. The culprit turned out to be the `is_update_available` function: ```shell num calls time self name ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1) 1 322.84 322.84 71.43% 322.84 322.84 71.43% is_update_available 2) 2 48.64 24.32 10.76% 48.64 24.32 10.76% compaudit ``` I was previously under the impression that checks for update only happen once every two weeks, but clearly a network call is made on every shell initialization. Here's a performance test between the same configurations with only the DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE parameter changed: ### DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE="false": ### DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE="true" ### Steps to reproduce 1. Put `zmodload zsh/zprof` at the start of .zshrc and `zprof` at the end of it 2. Open a new shell ### Expected behavior The shell shouldn't check for updates on every run ### Screenshots and recordings _No response_ ### OS / Linux distribution macOS 13.3 ### Zsh version 5.9 ### Terminal emulator Alacritty ### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL None ### Additional context This issue https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/11036 might be related

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

_No response_

### Describe the proposed feature.

1. Empty variable should be treated as null variable, not unset variable.

if I `export ZSH_THEME_AWS_PROFILE_PREFIX=""`, it expands to`<aws:` because of the following code:
```
${ZSH_THEME_AWS_PROFILE_PREFIX:=<aws:}
```

([here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/aws/aws.plugin.zsh#L202) and [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/aws/aws.plugin.zsh#L206))

2. Space between profile and region should be option, not mandatory.

```
_aws_to_show+=" "
```

I think it makes sense but it would be greater if I can remove or override this. I want something like `[admin/us-west-1]`.

([here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/aws/aws.plugin.zsh#L205))


### Describe alternatives you've considered

for feature 1 : use `-z` condition statement instead of `:=` expansion.

for feature 2 : make a space to optional (customizable) variable.

If moderators allow, I want to resolve this issue with my code.

author: roeniss

title: Blue triangle / artifact rendered after every `cd`
body: ### Describe the bug I am running Oh My Zsh (latest version: commit 5d3e86e2a48adf7a308773f8f1b725d187c7c5ef) on iTerm2. I am seeing a bug / change in behavior only when I run `tmux`. Each time I execute `cd ` I see an arrow-like character (a blue triangle) just before the prompt. The screenshot attached (`Screenshot 1`) shows this with an annotation. This artifact doesn't show up unless I'm running `tmux`. It also disappears when I detach and re-attach the same session, as you can see in `Screenshot 2` attached (but changing to another directory now again shows me the artifact on the next prompt). I was able to test out different commits in Oh My Zsh to determine that the difference in behavior is caused by commit 25c2c3a4683be60c3526858be960ee6ce1249245 (*feat(lib): send OSC 7 on directory change for more supported terminals (#9914)*). Specifically, the following line in `lib/termsupport.zsh` seems to be the ostensible culprit, as commenting it out fixes the issue: ``` add-zsh-hook precmd omz_termsupport_cwd ``` It's not obvious to me whether this is a bug in Oh My Zsh, or caused by some specific configuration in `tmux` not playing well with the change (I use Oh my tmux!, which configures most of tmux for me). ### Steps to reproduce 1. Open iTerm2. 2. Run `tmux` with a new session, or attach to an existing session. 3. Change to a directory that isn't the current one. 4. Immediately after the change is successful, notice the character rendered before the next prompt. ### Expected behavior Nothing surprising should be seen on Step 4 above. ### Screenshots and recordings The "blue triangle" in the first screenshot is the bug. I shouldn't be seeing these at all. This second screenshot shows the same terminal as above after I detached and re-attached the tmux session. The previously rendered "blue triangle"s have disappeared now. But if I change to another directory, it'll show up again before the subsequent prompt. ### OS / Linux distribution macOS ### Zsh version 5.9 ### Terminal emulator iTerm2 ### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL None ### Additional context * tmux 3.3a * [Oh my tmux!](https://github.com/gpakosz/.tmux) (Apr 12 2022, latest version)

title: Feature: Option to always show changelog on update
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

[This recent commit](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/029a6d2de8681d52142129eb8b6ff0f20be9f0a6) fixed the `interactive` option for the `update` command - but it also made it so that when I update I can't just automagically get the changelog displayed unless I actively remember to use the interactive option. And lets face it, I'm never going to remember that :p

### Describe the proposed feature.

A new option to implicitly use interactive mode when I run the `update` command, so I don't have to remember to use the interactive mode explicitly

Something like `zstyle ':omz:update' interactive true` (and `zstyle ':omz:update' interactive false` to require explicitly setting it, which will remain the default)

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I could just create a local alias for my own setup, if you don't want this as a built-in feature.... but I feel like other people will have gotten used to seeing the changelog automatically when using `update` so it may be desirable by more than just me to have this as a feature.

author: debiansid

title: Inconsistent `ls` alias, new window vs. omz reload; overwrites `alias ls='gls --color=tty'` only on reload
body:
### Describe the bug

relevant info:

- os: macOS `13.3.1`
- arch: `arm64`
- terminal application: iTerm2 3.5.0beta10
- shell: zsh 5.9, `/bin/zsh` (macOS built-in version)
- special config: using ["typewritten"](https://typewritten.dev) theme
- plugins: occurs with or without any plugins

---

Opening a new window vs. using `omz reload` (or entering the debug session with `zsh -xv 2> >(tee ~/omz-debug.log &>/dev/null)`, for that matter) creates inconsistent `ls` alias:

**new terminal window/tab**
```sh
> type -a ls
ls is an alias for ls -G
ls is /bin/ls
```

**after `omz reload`, `zsh`, `tmux` etc.**
```sh
> type -a ls
ls is an alias for gls --color=tty
ls is /bin/ls
```

this is problematic (at least on macOS) since there are several `ls` flags (such as `ls -@` which shows extended attributes) that result in error with `gls`.

I'm pretty sure the culprit is in [`lib/theme-and-appearance`,  lines 48-57](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/5d3e86e2a48adf7a308773f8f1b725d187c7c5ef/lib/theme-and-appearance.zsh#L48-L57):
```sh
  (darwin|freebsd)*)
    # This alias works by default just using $LSCOLORS
    test-ls-args ls -G && alias ls='ls -G'
    # Only use GNU ls if installed and there are user defaults for $LS_COLORS,
    # as the default coloring scheme is not very pretty
    [[ -n "$LS_COLORS" || -f "$HOME/.dircolors" ]] \
      && test-ls-args gls --color \
      && alias ls='gls --color=tty'
    ;;
  *)
```

NOTE: this does **not** occur when `DISABLE_LS_COLORS="true"`

why does this seem to run after invoking `zsh` but _not_ when `zsh` is initialized for a new window/tab?
it would be cool if there were a flag to turn off overwriting of builtin commands with coreutils variants _but still be able to have nice LS\_COLORS_

### Steps to reproduce

1. open a new terminal
2. run `which ls` or `type -a ls` or whatever
3. note the result
4. run any of the following: `omz reload`, `exec zsh`, `zsh`, `tmux`, `asciinema`
5. note that now `ls is an alias for gls --color=tty`

### Expected behavior

either `type -a ls` should be consistent with new window / omz reload OR ideally `gls` wouldn't be used by default on macOS for the reason that `gls` is missing some features unique to the macOS implementation (e.g. `-@`)

### Screenshots and recordings

unfortunately, it's impossible to reproduce this using `asciinema` because it runs a new `zsh` instance and so the `gls` alias is already used. here is a regular-ass video

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8978069/234725142-bef44c40-cf09-4a36-8a58-a14d8f778ed8.mp4

### workaround

manually set in `.zshrc` before `source ${OMZ}/oh-my-zsh.sh` (`LS_COLORS` might be different on your system)
```sh
export LS_COLORS="di=1;36:ln=35:so=32:pi=33:ex=31:bd=34;46:cd=34;43:su=30;41:sg=30;46:tw=30;42:ow=30;43"
alias ls='ls -G'
DISABLE_LS_COLORS="true"
```

### OS / Linux distribution

macOS 13.3.1

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (5d3e86e)

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2 3.5.0beta10

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

using theme `typewritten` but doesn't seem to interfere.

title: Cannot save scratch buffer (fzf plugin)
body:
### Describe the bug

Hello there!
I guess there is an issue with fzf plugin. My goal is to edit a file from a fzf list. So I've added a binding `--bind 'ctrl-e:execute(micro {+})'`.

It works as expected for this workflow (without using a fzf plugin):
- type `fzf`, press `Enter`
- select a target file
- hit `Ctrl-E`
- edit a file in `micro` editor, save it

But it doesn't work if I use fzf plugin:

- hit `Ctrl-T` to run `fzf` (<-- the only difference)
- select a target file
- hit `Ctrl-E` to open an editor
- edit a file, save it.

Here at the last "save" step I've got an error `Cannot save scratch buffer`. For vim I will get an error `Vim: Warning: Output is not to a terminal`.
I use only 2 plugins: z, fzf.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Add a binding to edit a file from fzf list. Add `--bind 'ctrl-e:execute(micro {+})'` to `FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS` in `.zshrc`. Feel free to use any editor (`vim`, `$EDITOR`, etc).
2. Hit `Ctrl-T` to run `fzf`
3. Select a file, hit `Ctrl-E` to open it in an editor
4. Make some changes, save the file.

You'll get an error `Cannot save scratch buffer` for `micro` editor, or `Vim: Warning: Output is not to a terminal` from `vim` editor.

### Expected behavior

It should save a file with changes. The same way like it saves if you type `fzf` to call fzf (at the first step).

Manjaro Linux

author: kejianhong

title: Better handling of ZSH in emulation mode
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

ZSH has the ability to emulate bash/ksh/sh either by the `emulate` command or by argv[0] starting with sh, ksh, or bash. This causes omz to error constantly because the modules are not automatically loaded, the syntax is different, and various other issues.

When `oh-my-zsh.sh` is loaded with a non-ZSH shell, it gives a nice error message, but when zsh is in emulation mode and the script is manually loaded (since in emulation mode it won't load .zshrc) you get
```
$ exec -a bash zsh -l
$ . .oh-my-zsh/oh-my-zsh.sh
.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:13: command not found: zstyle
(anon):40: command not found: zstyle
compinit:141: parse error: condition expected: $1
_omz_source:10: command not found: zstyle
_omz_source:15: bad set of key/value pairs for associative array
```

### Describe the proposed feature.

A simple check for `[ "$(emulate)" = zsh ]` could give a nicer error message.

If it is detected when the script is loaded, it could say
```
Error: Oh My Zsh can't be loaded in emulation mode.
This can happen if the emulate command is run, or zsh is renamed to sh, bash, or ksh.
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternatively it could try to manually escape emulation mode, but that breaks other things.

### Additional context

_No response_

### Related Issues

_No response_

author: bittner

title: vi-mode incorrect cursor style when exiting visual mode
body:
### Describe the bug

When switching out of visual mode, the cursor does not change back to a block.

### Steps to reproduce

Minimal config:
```zsh
# .zshrc
...
plugins+=(vi-mode)

VI_MODE_SET_CURSOR=true
...
```
1. Enable the `vi-mode` plugin: `plugins+=(vi-mode)`
2. Set `VI_MODE_SET_CURSOR=true` in `.zshrc`
3. Enter visual select and hit escape
4. The cursor doesn't change back to a block as it should in normal mode



### Expected behavior

I would expect the cursor to remain a block when entering visual mode (or exiting visual mode) instead of switching into a vertical line. 

### Screenshots and recordings

![Screen Recording 2023-05-21 at 6 05 46 PM](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/assets/56745535/7bd0af0a-6172-45e8-9282-b0cf6c63dec3)


### OS / Linux distribution

macOS 13.3

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (b06663d)

### Terminal emulator

kitty

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

This seems to have been introduced by the fix for #11586 as removing the following lines resolves the issue:
```zsh
function _visual-mode {
  typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=visual
  _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap "$VI_KEYMAP"
  zle .visual-mode
}
zle -N visual-mode _visual-mode
```

Bureau theme

author: pvelesko

title: The option to disable Oh My Zsh before running zshell
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

I'm a member of [VS Code extension for Ruby debugger](https://github.com/ruby/vscode-rdbg). Currently, we have a problem of https://github.com/ruby/vscode-rdbg/issues/277. The problem is that we can't extract the result from stdout since Oh My Zsh outputs many strings to stdout. As a process, we get the result of running a command in the new process. Here is an example. In this case, we want to get the path, but it's hard to extract it. 

## Example Code
```ts
const p = child_process.exec(<command>);
let path: string;
p.stdout?.on("data", out => {
  console.log(out)
});
```

## Actual Outputs
```
"\u001b]1337;RemoteHost=miharekar@iMac-Pro.local\u0007\u001b]1337;CurrentDir=/Users/miharekar/Development/personal/rdbg\u0007\u001b]1337;ShellIntegrationVersion=6;shell=zsh\u0007/var/folders/x1/h2b59yq14cs6pv9r_x1_39lw0000gn/T/ruby-debug-sock-501/ruby-debug-miharekar-12243"
```

## Expected Outputs

```
"/var/folders/x1/h2b59yq14cs6pv9r_x1_39lw0000gn/T/ruby-debug-sock-501/ruby-debug-miharekar-12243"
```

### Describe the proposed feature.

It would be great to add the option to disable Oh My Zsh by setting environment variables, etc.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

### Related Issues

_No response_

author: rhoitjadhav

title: nvm lazy load not working
body:
### Describe the bug

Using `zstyle ':omz:plugins:nvm' lazy yes` in `.zshrc` throws an error when opening a new terminal (and corrupts nvm auto version detection feature):

```
load-nvmrc:2: command not found: nvm_find_nvmrc
```

### Steps to reproduce

I installed oh-my-zsh on my M1 mac with

```
sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
```

Then I installed `nvm` by documentation with

```
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.3/install.sh | bash
```

As documented I then update the `.zshrc` configuration on/with the following lines:

```
# Which plugins would you like to load?
# Standard plugins can be found in $ZSH/plugins/
# Custom plugins may be added to $ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/
# Example format: plugins=(rails git textmate ruby lighthouse)
# Add wisely, as too many plugins slow down shell startup.
plugins=(git nvm)

zstyle ':omz:plugins:nvm' lazy yes
zstyle ':omz:plugins:nvm' autoload yes

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

Opening a new terminal tab now throws the error `load-nvmrc:2: command not found: nvm_find_nvmrc`.

Removing the `zstyle ':omz:plugins:nvm' lazy yes` line, nvm is not lazy loaded anymore, but everything else works as expected.

```
Found '/Users/*****/Projects/****/****-frontend/.nvmrc' with version <18>
Now using node v18.16.0 (npm v9.5.1)
```

### Expected behavior

The provided auto loader works without errors.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

macOS 13.4

### Zsh version

5.9

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2, Warp

None

_No response_

author: andytael

title: gunwipall cluttering the reflog
body:
### Describe the bug

Because of the way guwipall has been inplemented in  #11725 , one git reset command gets executed for each wip commit. 

This isn't ideal if we need to go back, as the reflog may get filled with resets 

I'm unable to send a PR at this time, but the following should do the trick 


```shell
commit=$(git rev-list --grep='--wip--' --invert-grep --max-count=1 HEAD)

# Check if a commit without "--wip--" was found and it's not the same as HEAD
if [[ "$commit" != "$(git rev-parse HEAD)" ]]; then
  git reset $commit || return 1
fi
```

### Steps to reproduce

Make a git repository with a few wip commits
1. git init 
2. git commit --allow-empty -m "init"
3. gwip --allow-empty  a few times
4. gunwipall 
5. git reflog 
6. The reflog is filled with resets

### Expected behavior

Ideally, only one reset should be done 

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

N/A

5.9

master (cb8b677)

title: Docker CLI generated completions not working for CLI sub-commands
body:
### Describe the bug

The docker cli generated completions for ZSH are not working with OMZSH when using one the plugins (subcommand). 

However, the completions work when disabling OMZSH (plain ZSH). And I have even tried the bash version and they work perfectly.


This bug has no relation to the `docker` or `docker-compose` plugin for OMZSH. I refer to the completions generated by Docker CLI itself. 

### Steps to reproduce

1. You can generate completions for ZSH with:
```console
$ docker completion zsh > .oh-my-zsh/completions/_docker
```
2. remove any file `.zcompdump*`
3. Restart the shell.
4.  try getting auto completions out of a plugin like the compose plugin with `docker compose <TAB>`

Completions should appear.

_No response_

author: Tom89757

title: Lost setopt auto_cd at some point
body:
### Describe the bug

At some point in, the last few months, I lost `auto_cd` and have had to `setopt auto_cd` after oh-my-zsh initializes. After digging around, I noticed this commit which moved `setopt auto_cd`: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/277f38212aef31a6baba2cf1a0a355af611be5e0

@mcornella responded to my comment here: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/277f38212aef31a6baba2cf1a0a355af611be5e0#commitcomment-114465702

> Everything else ruled out, you can run zsh in trace mode and capture a trace of the startup process with `zsh -xvic exit &>~/omz-debug.log`, and then inspect that file to see if `autocd` is in there and is later `unsetopt`-ed, or whether `lib/directories.zsh` gets loaded at all (it should be loaded as part of in `oh-my-zsh.sh`).
>
> Feel free to open a new issue if you're unsure how to investigate or can't see anything abnormal.

I've confirmed that I do see `setopt auto_cd` and I do not see `unsetopt auto_cd`. I see the `_omz_source lib/directories.zsh` call which is invoking this.

I also seem to be missing other settings. My current `setopts`:

```
$ setopt
nobgnice
combiningchars
completeinword
correct
extendedhistory
histignorealldups
histreduceblanks
histverify
nohup
ignoreeof
incappendhistory
interactive
nolistbeep
localoptions
localtraps
login
monitor
promptsubst
sharehistory
shinstdin
zle
```

Do you have any pro tips on troubleshooting this further? Currently, I can just add `setopt auto_cd` to get around this and I can just keep moving that up the stack to drill down to where this might be happening. 

Thanks!

### Steps to reproduce

I use https://github.com/justinharringa/dotfiles#install which effectively bootstraps this for my `.zshrc`: https://github.com/justinharringa/dotfiles/blob/master/zsh/zshrc.symlink

author: Theiaz

title: The 3rd installation option fails. Recommendation is to remove it
body:
### Describe the bug

This is about the 3rd installation method in the table in the ReadMe:

fetch | sh -c "$(fetch -o - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
-- | --

I had not been aware of the `fetch` command, so I downloaded it via Homebrew (thinking it was from GNU)

This method fails. 
```
sh -c "$(fetch -o - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
```

yields this error output:
```
sh: Incorrect: command not found
sh: line 2: NAME:: command not found
ERRO[2023-07-05T21:13:00-07:00] The --repo flag is required. Run "fetch --help" for full usage info.  binary=fetch version=
sh: line 5: USAGE:: command not found
sh: -c: line 6: syntax error near unexpected token `newline'
sh: -c: line 6: `   fetch [global options] <local-download-path>'
```

Looking into this further I see the code is owned by [Gruntwork](https://www.gruntwork.io/)

Repo: [gruntwork-io/fetch](https://github.com/gruntwork-io/fetch)

The  [fetch examples](https://github.com/gruntwork-io/fetch#examples) that express the current way to use `fetch` is not reflected in the invocation in the installation table. 

Nearly every time I tried `fetch` I saw this:
```
The --repo flag is required.
```

And when I finally found syntax that began to work, I finally stopped at this last invocation which apparently downloaded the entire repo as if I had run `git clone`. 

```
# fetch --repo="https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh" --branch=master --ref="tools/install.sh" install.sh
INFO[2023-07-05T21:04:39-07:00] Downloading latest commit from branch "master" of https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh ...  binary=fetch version=
INFO[2023-07-05T21:04:39-07:00] Extracting files from <repo>/ to install.sh ...  binary=fetch version=
INFO[2023-07-05T21:04:40-07:00] 995 files extracted                           binary=fetch version=
INFO[2023-07-05T21:04:40-07:00] Download and file extraction complete.        binary=fetch version=

```
I could not find the exact correct invocation. Prolly too much trouble. :-)

This is another error or warning that notifies the User that they may be violating the owner's prescribed license for usage. I looked no further. 
```
Assuming GitHub Enterprise since the provided url (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh) does not appear to be for GitHub.com
```

Perhaps it is a good idea to remove this open source wart. 

HTH!


### Steps to reproduce

```
sh -c "$(fetch -o - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
```


### Expected behavior

expected not to fail

### Screenshots and recordings

n/a

body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

Docker plugin

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

[plugins/docker/docker.plugin.zsh](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/fe4b5659863c388786986d70fa6d1bb66b00afb6/plugins/docker/docker.plugin.zsh#L49)
```zsh
# ...
  if is-at-least 23.0.0 $_docker_version; then
    # If the completion file doesn't exist yet, we need to autoload it and
    # bind it to `docker`. Otherwise, compinit will have already done that.
    if [[ ! -f "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker" ]]; then
      typeset -g -A _comps
      autoload -Uz _docker
      _comps[docker]=_docker
    fi
    command docker completion zsh >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker"
  else
    command cp "${0:h}/completions/_docker" "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker"
  fi
# ...
```
Due to the line number 49 of `docker.plugin.zsh`, the file `~/.oh-my-zsh/cache/completions/_docker`  is overwritten with `$(docker completion zsh)` command which doesn't return many things as output (about 100 lines of zsh script). I simply just removed the condition above, so it seems to work now. I just want to ask if this condition is really necessary?

# For people who may get this issue:
 Try to modify the line number 49 of `~/.oh-my-zsh/cache/completions/plugins/docker/docker.plugin.zsh`


### Describe the proposed feature.

the file named `~/.oh-my-zsh/cache/completions/_docker ` should be written correctly

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

title: Slow startup due to `git --version` in `check_for_upgrade.sh`
body:
### Describe the bug

ohmyzsh run `git --version` in its `check_for_upgrade.sh` startup script.  On recent versions of macOS, this command can hang for a very long time under certain circumstances (like after a reboot).

This isn't caused by a license that hasn't been accepted yet (as is the case after an update), and is not permanently fixed by `sudo xcodebuild -license accept`.  It can recur after a reboot, or after system updates that don't affect Xcode.  It's stupid, but that's how it is.

Can we skip the `git --version` check, or add some workaround for macOS/system `git`?

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install Xcode developer tools and accept license, so that system `zsh` and `git` work
2. Install `ohmyzsh`
3. Restart computer, open Terminal (sometimes)

### Expected behavior

`ohmyzsh` startup should never hang for 10s of seconds, because `git --version` should never hang for 10s of seconds.

### Screenshots and recordings

macOS 13 (latest)

title: Problem when oh-my-zsh getting current branch name
body:
### Describe the bug

I have a branch named *代码更新*, but oh-my-zsh display it as *代�*. The character '码' became '�' and all characters behind it disappered.

I think it might due to this code `ref=$(__git_prompt_git symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2> /dev/null)`  which was used in the `git_prompt_info` function in *.oh-my-zsh/lib/git.sh*.

When the branch name contains a character in unicode range 77A0 - 783F (from my testing) like '码', '砀', this problem happens,  the command `git symbolic-ref --short HEAD` seems cannot handle characters in this range.

Then I replaced the code to `ref=$(__git_prompt_git branch --show-current 2> /dev/null)`, it works well.

### Steps to reproduce

1. create a branch named *代码更新*
2. checkout the branch *代码更新*

### Expected behavior

oh-my-zsh was expected to display:
➜  my-awesome-project git:(代码更新)

title: Hardcoded tmux config file
body:
### Describe the bug

the tmux plugin harcoded the configuration file path to ~/.tmux.conf when not provided. this blocks tmux from using the [XDG Base Dirs spec](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-latest.html) which was recently (and after long discussions) merged 

### Steps to reproduce

1. Enable the plugin 'tmux' 
2. set the plugin to autostart
3. create a tmux.conf file in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/tmux/tmux.conf
4. start a shell 
5. 

### Expected behavior

tmux should pick up the file in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/tmux/tmux.conf and apply that configuration

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS (but this is not distro specific)

### Zsh version

5.8.1 (but it's not relevant)

### Terminal emulator

alacritty

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

Here

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/tmux/tmux.plugin.zsh#L29

$ZSH_TMUX_CONFIG should be left empty when not provided by the user so that this condition

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/8bdb5c959c9a5c74d3b59c05a3a0bca5e602c3cd/plugins/tmux/tmux.plugin.zsh#L86C7

is skipped and no `-f` option is added to the command line

adding 

```
export ZSH_TMUX_CONFIG=${HOME}/.config/tmux/tmux.conf
```

of course fixes the problem but this should not be necessary

author: MoonBunnyZZZ

title: Some themes cause phantom characters to be appended to the end of the prompt (temporarily) upon zsh doing the directory listing for autocomplete (only in some environments))
body:
### Describe the bug

When some themes are applied in OhMyZsh when it is run in certain environments, they can cause a bug that results in the duplication of some number of the first characters in the user typed command when zsh lists the files in the directory for the user to choose from for a completion. Here is an example of the behavior with the theme `refined` enabled:

1. The user types a command that will result in a directory listing by zsh: 
```
~
❯ ls /var/                                                               
```
2. The user presses tab:
```
~
❯ lsls /var/   
backups/  lib/      lock@     mail/     run@      tmp/
cache/    local/    log/      opt/      spool/    www/
```
3. The user backspaces as much as possible (the generated phantom characters can't be deleted):
```
~
❯ ls                                                                       
backups/  lib/      lock@     mail/     run@      tmp/
cache/    local/    log/      opt/      spool/    www/
```
4. The user presses enter (nothing happens, showing how the shell doesn't actually receive these characters):
```
~
❯ ls                                                                       

~
❯                                                                        
```

Some themes (such as fwalch, adben, and jreese to name a few I found using random) are not affected by this bug.

Some other themes, like agnoster, are affected worse with the first 5 characters being replicated as phantom characters!

My testing also reveled that this bug only occurs in some environments. I found this bug to be present when:
- Launching zsh within the chroot created by artix-chroot (but a normal chroot works fine)
- Launching zsh by suing to a user using doas (but sudo and normal su work fine)

### Steps to reproduce

1. Use the default OhMyZsh settings, but change the theme to an affected one (ex. refined)
2. Install opendoas and run `echo "permit nopass :root" | sudo tee /etc/doas.conf` to allow using doas from root without a password
3. Su to root somehow
4. From root, use doas to su to the user that has OhMyZsh installed (ex. `doas su user`)
5. Type `ls /var/` (or some other directory with sub-directories) and press tab
6. See the phantom characters

### Expected behavior

No phantom characters appear after pressing tab. 

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Both Void Linux and Artix Linux

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (7d5e125)

### Terminal emulator

Linux TTY

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

_No response_

author: slavag

title: ssh-agent plugin should fail gracefully and better handle edge cases.
body:
### Describe the bug

There are currently two failures modes which should be better addresses in the [`ssh-agent` plugin](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/tree/master/plugins/ssh-agent).

1. [In the event you do **NOT** have ssh-agent and you have the plugin enabled](https://unix.stackexchange.com/q/753314/3285), a more desirable error would read "You have enabled the `ssh-agent` plugin for ohmyzsh which requires `ssh-agent` which is not installed."

2. [In the event you do **NOT** have a `~/.ssh` directory](https://unix.stackexchange.com/q/753316/3285), a more desirable would read _"You have enabled the `ssh-agent` which requires a directory be created called `~/.ssh` to store a cache file. This directory does not exist, please create it."_ Alternatively, I suppose we could just create the `~/.ssh` directory with perms 700.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Enable plugin
2. Log in without `ssh-agent` for the first obscure error, or without an `~/.ssh` directory for the second obscure error.

### Expected behavior

Better error messages.

fatal: not a git repository (or any parent up to mount point /)

title: Docker logs tab plugin fails with custom docker ps format
body:
### Describe the bug

Previously with OhMyZsh, I was able to use tab to auto-complete docker container names when typing `docker logs my-service-name-fo...` etc.

However, this functionality broke after applying a custom psFormat format in `~/.docker/config.json`.

My suggestion is, if hitting tab is internally processing the output of `docker ps` for the active container names, that the internal command instead also uses its own hard-coded format, such as: `docker ps --format='{{.Names}}'`

My `config.json` is configured as follows:
```json
{
  "psFormat": "table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Command}}"
}
```

You can view all configurable options using this command:
`docker ps --format='{{json .}}'`

### Steps to reproduce

1. Type docker logs <partial-container-name>
2. Hit tab to check auto-complete works
3. Implement a custom docker ps format in  `~/.docker/config.json`, where the name column is moved
4. Check docker ps has updated
5. Type docker logs <partial-container-name>
6. Hit tab and observe auto-complete fails

### Expected behavior

Auto-complete works no matter the user's custom PS format 

### OS / Linux distribution

CentOS 9

### Zsh version

5.8

### Terminal emulator

GNOME Terminal

Extends battery plugin support for OpenBSD

### Describe the proposed feature.

The battery plugin has support for FreeBSD through the sysctl hw.acpi* commands. But similar approach can be taken to extend support for OpenBSD using the [hw.sensors.acpibat*](https://man.openbsd.org/acpibat.4) commands.

But in OpenBSD the [apm](https://man.openbsd.org/apm.8) tool is more user friendly and works out of the box unlike the former which requires us to calculate percentage from power usage.

author: im-notpua

title: Secrets in History!
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

Hello, people.
Thank you for this great and fantastic shell !

Anyway, I've an idea for improve ZSH. It's little like security improve or something like this, but not exactly. 
Sometimes, we want to use a personal or secret phrase in our command line. For example: 
`[ZSH sample@host ]$  program --user IamUser --password 1234 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7795  `
Here, I've a secret password. But ZSH like other shells adds this command in history.\
`[ZSH sample@host ]$  history`
`1    program --user IamUser --password 1234 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7795`
And I want make my password hidden in my history.



### Describe the proposed feature.

Idea !
For example, we can use a 'secret' tag for hide password:
`[ZSH sample@host ]$  program --user IamUser --password <sec>1234<ret> --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7795  `
After that, history is like this:
`[ZSH sample@host ]$  history`
`1    program --user IamUser --password 1234 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7795`
`2    program --user IamUser --password SECRET --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7795`

Have we had a tool or plugin do that? 
If we haven't, let's create it !

Have a nice time! 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

_No response_

title: feat(termsupport): Allow configurable terminal title patterns & cmdline truncation limits
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

~_No response_~
N/A: Code is in core / [`lib/termsupport.zsh`](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/ccce2e1cfdf5b9680f691a402e288d9cf6ce272a/lib/termsupport.zsh#L1-L110)

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

### Context for Use-Case:

Desktop environments that use tiling window managers (e.g. [i3][2], [sway][3], [hyprland][4], [river][5], and others) depend on window title for various things such as [window matching rules][6], [tab][7] & [workspace icons][8], [taskbar title rewriting][9], etc...  Adding icons based on window title makes it a lot easier and faster to visually pick out a window amongst many others.

Allowing for configurable window title would be a huge usability and UI/UX improvement for these desktop environments.  Given the above context, would you be willing to agree that allowing title customization would improve UI/UX for those environments?

### Describe the proposed feature.

Allow for configurable `ZSH_THEME_TERM_*TITLE*` settings, similar to proposal in #11870 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternative feature proposal was in #11870

### Additional context

@mcornella:

Sorry for opening a new issue but GitHub would not allow me to respond in #11870 after it was closed.  So I'll try to respond to your comments here instead, and Thanks for the feedback!

> Doing so requires a level of hacky-ness that I don't really feel worth it or stylish, and is very coupled with the title logic.

Yeah, that's fair.  While I'd agree that POSIX shell syntax isn't the most stylish of languages in general... I'm not sure I'd agree that [setting a default if unset][1] is all that "hacky" per se...  Although I would agree that the "`${foo:-default-here}`" syntax when combined with ZSH's [Prompt Expansion / truncation behaviour][zsh-prompt-expansion] syntax together is rather esoteric.  It's a bit unfortunate that it starts to become less readable and look more like Perl than shell scripting 😂.  Perhaps another solution to make it more clear and readable might be to split it up into multiple lines?

For example:

```zsh
# Allow overriding these settings, but fallback to defaults if unset
...

_default_tab_title_idle='%15<..<%~%<<' #15 char left truncated PWD
ZSH_THEME_TERM_TAB_TITLE_IDLE="${ZSH_THEME_TERM_TAB_TITLE_IDLE:-$_default_tab_title_idle}"

...  # Do the same pattern for the rest
```

@mcornella, @carlosala:

> ... I think at this stage we are not looking at getting at such a fine-grained level of customization.
> ...
> We'll be looking in the future to revise this decision to see if we can improve this somehow.

I do hope this will be possible 😅

When I was looking at the `lib/termsupport.zsh` in #11868, I noticed that it seemed a bit strange for these variables being hard-coded and named `ZSH_THEME_TERM_*` as if the _original intent_ was to expose them as configurable theme settings.  I figured a quick fix would be to use the "set if unset" POSIX syntax, but maybe there's a better more stylish way? `¯\_(ツ)_/¯`


>  In the meantime, you can redefine the functions yourself in your zshrc file, or disable our solution by setting `DISABLE_AUTO_TITLE=true` and fully customize your logic while keeping compatibility with the title function.

Unfortunately, there are already [forked older versions of omz's title set behavior][manjaro-termsupport-fork] which are currently broken and don't actually set the title based on current running command.  Overriding both OMZ's `title` & `precmd` / `preexec` hooks and the copied & broken versions clutters and slows down the pre-installed Zsh hooks on these distros.  So far I've tried overriding both, but the result was not ideal.  My current workaround was to edit the OS-supplied file to disable their broken hooks, to get stock OMZ behavior only to find that this also didn't work for my use case requirements:

- Idle title with current working directory & longer truncation char limit
- Running command as title when not idle

It's rather unfortunate that the title setting behavior in OMZ isn't configurable and has a 15 character limit hard-coded by default.  Otherwise, this would have worked without modification to OMZ's internals.



[1]: https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009604499/utilities/xcu_chap02.html#tag_02_06_02
[2]: https://i3wm.org/
[3]: https://swaywm.org/
[4]: https://hyprland.org/
[5]: https://github.com/riverwm/river
[6]: https://i3wm.org/docs/userguide.html#for_window
[7]: https://github.com/allie-wake-up/swaycons#why
[8]: https://github.com/Lyr-7D1h/swayest_workstyle#swayest-workstyle
[9]: https://github.com/Alexays/Waybar/wiki/Module:-Taskbar#rewrite-rules

[zsh-prompt-expansion]: https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Prompt-Expansion.html#Conditional-Substrings-in-Prompts
[manjaro-termsupport-fork]: https://github.com/Chrysostomus/manjaro-zsh-config/issues/46

### Related Issues

- #11870: Proposed PR for this feature implementation (Closed, but couldn't respond there)

title: Fn 0 doesn't work for slash
body:
### Describe the bug

Maybe reopening [#8211](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/8211).

I'm used to press `Fn + 0` to type `/` anywhere as I use a notebook.  
This is easier than:
1. Enabling NumLk
2. Pressing 0
3. Disabling NumLk

It works everywhere in any system.  
I've did it in **Bash**, _text boxes_, **Fish**, and also in **Zsh**.

But it doesn't work in oh-my-zsh.  
(And no, it's not about my terminal emulator)

The issue [#8211](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/8211) was marked as `not our issue`, but I think that if it only happens in oh-my-zsh it is a unsupportable feature and should be supported even as a non-default feature.

I tried omz on Alacritty terminal and it worked fine.  
But anyway I think omz should support xfce-terminal.

### Steps to reproduce

Pressing `Fn + 0` (nothing happens...)

Appear `0` is pressed so, and appear `/` it pressed `Fn + 0`.

author: Ostefanini

title: Conventional commit aliases from `git-commit` plugin fail due to non POSIX syntax
body:
### Describe the bug

[Git uses `sh` as its default shell, with no possibility to change it.](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73162225/how-to-make-bash-as-default-shell-in-git-alias)

The recently created `git-commit` plugin for conventional commits adds git aliases which contain `[[` to check for conditions.
[This is non POSIX syntax](https://askubuntu.com/a/1277981) that `sh` does not recognise (but `bash` and `zsh` do.) One should use `[` instead when working with `sh`.

Offending line in the plugin:
https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/079dbff2c4f22935a71101c511e2285327d8ab68/plugins/git-commit/git-commit.plugin.zsh#L3


### Steps to reproduce

1. Add the plugin `plugins+=(git-commit)`
2. Reload with `omz reload`
3. `cd` into a git repository
4. Run an alias: `git feat --scope "zshrc/git-commit" "add plugin for conventional commits"`
5. Error: `error: pathspec 'something' did not match any file(s) known to git`

### Expected behavior

The alias should work properly.

### Screenshots and recordings


Terminal output:
```zsh
$ git feat --scope "zshrc/git-commit" "add plugin for conventional commits"
a() { if [[ "$1" == "-s" || "$1" == "--scope" ]]; then git commit -m "feat(${2}): ${@:3}"; else git commit -m "feat: ${@}"; fi }; a: 1: [[: not found
a() { if [[ "$1" == "-s" || "$1" == "--scope" ]]; then git commit -m "feat(${2}): ${@:3}"; else git commit -m "feat: ${@}"; fi }; a: 1: --scope: not found
error: pathspec 'zshrc/git-commit' did not match any file(s) known to git
error: pathspec 'add plugin for conventional commits' did not match any file(s) known to git
```

### OS / Linux distribution

Ubuntu 20.04

### Zsh version

5.8

### Oh My Zsh version

master (079dbff)

### Terminal emulator

Windows Terminal (1.17.11461.0)

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

WSL2

### Additional context

I suspect that the creator of this plugin set up a symlink to point `sh` to `bash` on their machine and didn't realise that this was not the default for most people. Will open a PR to fix it.

author: karanchhatwani

title: Gradle plugin should not lookup `settings.gradle` and `settings.gradle.kts`
body:
Consider the following case:

```
gradle/
  wrapper/
plugin/
  build.gradle
  settings.gradle
build.gradle
gradlew
gradlew.bat
settings.gradle
```

```
# settings.gradle
pluginManagement {
    includeBuild('plugin/')
}
```

In the above case, it is not possible to call the `gradle` alias when I'm in the `plugin` directory, for the reason that the script breaks the search for the `gradlew` script because it has found the `settings.gradle` file. In the following condition, the script should only check for the existence of the `gradlew` file and possibly whether it has the executable bit set, but nothing more.

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/0e1e877ea9182f87b3ab34cbc2ea944b34f5345e/plugins/gradle/gradle.plugin.zsh#L9

Greetings
Piotr Minkina

author: abuhabban

title: Autocorrect command doesn't detect modified/relative path
body:
### Describe the bug

I've added `./node_modules/.bin` to my path. This works, but I get constant autocorrect command prompts:
```
╰─ tsc
zsh: correct 'tsc' to 'tc' [nyae]? n

╰─ tsc
zsh: correct 'tsc' to 'tc' [nyae]? n
```

I have fiddled with various options like:
- `setopt hash_list_all`
- `setopt hash_cmds`
- `setopt hash_dirs`
- `zstyle -e ':completion:*' command-path 'reply=( "$PWD/node_modules/.bin" "$path[@]" )'` (suggeted [here](https://superuser.com/questions/975946/zsh-autocomplete-relative-paths))
- `zstyle ":completion:*:commands" rehash 1` (suggested [here](https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/444834/dont-try-to-correct-a-command-that-now-exists))

Nothing I've tried works. Even with a manual rehash. But when I say `n`, it executes successfully. I also get green highlighting, so zsh seems to know the path exists.

### Steps to reproduce

1. add `./node_modules/.bin` to path (same behavior with leading ./ omitted)
2. cd to a folder with a `node_modules/.bin` directory
3. try executing a command (e.g. `tsc`) that exists in the node_modules bin
4. When prompted to run a different command, say no
5. Repeat step 3

### Expected behavior

Since my PATH variable includes a relative path, I shouldn't get command autocorrect prompts for commands that exist on my path. Or, at least, I shouldn't get it if I run the same command more than once (I.e. step 5 shouldn't prompt a correction again).

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Windows 11 WSL, Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS

### Zsh version

5.8.1

### Terminal emulator

Windows Terminal

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

WSL2

### Additional context
The only solution I've found that works is to hardcode the following: `CORRECT_IGNORE="tc|tac"`, which is undesirable, because if `tsc` doesn't exist on the path, I would probably want it to suggest `tc` or `tac`...

title: `dirhistory` doesn't preserve future directories on `cd`
body:
### Describe the bug

> Say the user has navigated the directory history such that the past directories are (/tmp /tmp/a) and the future directories are (/usr /usr/bin). At this point if the user did 'cd /bin', then the past directories would become (/tmp /tmp/a /bin). That is, the future directories (/usr /usr/bin) are lost.

author: mcornella

title: Debian plugin variable override clarification
body:
### Describe the bug

The readme for the debian plugin states:

>     $apt_pref: use aptitude or apt if installed, fallback is apt-get.
>     $apt_upgr: use upgrade or safe-upgrade (for aptitude).
> 
> Set $apt_pref and $apt_upgr to whatever command you want (before sourcing Oh My Zsh) to override this behavior.

If only one of the variables are set prior to sourcing, it will be overridden. Either explicitly state that both need to be set in the readme, or, preferably, change the behavior. E.g. like this:

```
if [[ -z $apt_pref || -z $apt_upgr ]]; then
    if [[ -z $apt_pref ]]; then	 
    	if [[ -e $commands[aptitude] ]]; then
      	  apt_pref='aptitude'
    	elif [[ -e $commands[apt] ]]; then
     	   apt_pref='apt'
    	else
      	  apt_pref='apt-get'
    	fi
    fi
    if [[ -z $apt_upgr ]]; then	 
    	if [[ -e $commands[aptitude] ]]; then
      	  apt_upgr='safe-upgrade'
    	elif [[ -e $commands[apt] ]]; then
     	   apt_upgr='upgrade'
    	else
      	  apt_upgr='upgrade'
    	fi
    fi
fi
```

### Steps to reproduce

1. Enable 'debian'
2. Set either _apt_pref_ OR _apt_upgr_ to something other than the default
3. Start zsh
4. The setting is now overridden

### Expected behavior

Readme should clarify that both variables need to be set, or plugin should respect the variable set

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

MX Linux 23

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (6768906)

### Terminal emulator

xfce4-terminal

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

_No response_

author: gitKrystan

title: common-aliases causes extra returns
body:
### Describe the bug

When opening the terminal, the prompt typically looks like this:

```sh
Last login: Thu Oct 12 19:54:00 on ttys000
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
```

After editing `~/.zshrc` and adding [common-aliases] to the plugins, the terminal now adds some unnecessary returns:

```sh
Last login: Thu Oct 12 19:56:40 on ttys002
                                                                                                                                                                          

user@host ~ %  # this one naturally occurs from opening the terminal
                                                                                                                                                                          

user@host ~ % # this one is me just hitting the ENTER key
                                                                                                                                                                          

user@host ~ % 
```

It's worth noting that this does NOT happen in iTerm2. 

### Steps to reproduce

1. Enable plugin: `common-aliases`
2. `:w ~/.zshrc`
3. Open a new Terminal window
4. Fail: the additional lines are introduced

### Expected behavior

The prompt looks as preferred:

```sh
Last login: Thu Oct 12 19:54:00 on ttys000
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
user@host ~ % 
```

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

macOS/13.5.2 (Venura)

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (b1821a2)

### Terminal emulator

Terminal.app

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

Thank you!

body:
### Describe the bug

I using pip to install rembg by using command `pip install rembg[gpu,cli]` but is isn't work.
```
❯ pip install rembg[gpu,cli]
zsh: no matches found: rembg[gpu,cli]
```
I switch to bash shell and run command `pip install rembg[gpu,cli]` again and it work.



### Steps to reproduce

1. install zsh
2. install omz
3. install [anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download)
4. create conda env by using command `conda create -n py10 python=3.10`
5. activate env by command `conda activate py10`
6. run command 'pip install rembg[gpu,cli]'

### Expected behavior

Console log when using ***zsh***
```
❯ pip install rembg[gpu,cli]
zsh: no matches found: rembg[gpu,cli]
```

author: knotgrass

title: Aliases returning an error
body:
### Describe the bug

I am using the Aliases plugin, and it is returning an error on basic usage. 

```
╰─$ als       
als: missing archive argument
```

### Steps to reproduce

Enable plugin
Type `als`


### Expected behavior

Should return all aliases.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Mac OS Sonomo 14.0

### Zsh version

5.9

### Terminal emulator

Visual Studio Code

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

author: alicanb

title: Add plugin for advanced history search
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

_No response_

### Describe the proposed feature.

A plugin that adds functionality similar to this: https://github.com/astralarya/history

# history

Longterm bash history with advanced search and select.

Manages a comprehensive history file that
allows searching and rerunning commands according to
executed command, working directory, time run, user, or host.


## Usage

* **h**[!] [*CONTEXT*] [*TIMESPEC*] [--] [*SEARCH*]...
  * Search history for pattern
* **dh**[!] [*CONTEXT*] [*TIMESPEC*] [--] [*SEARCH*]...
  * Show history of commands in this directory and subdirectories and optionally filter with pattern
* **ldh**[!] [*CONTEXT*] [*TIMESPEC*] [--] [*SEARCH*]...
  * Show history of commands in this directory only and optionally filter with pattern

### SEARCH
SEARCH matches the executed command.
It is interpreted as a `gawk` regular expression.
Multiple SEARCH arguments are joined with `.*`.

### TIMESPEC
TIMESPEC matches the timestamp and
is an argument of the form "[START..END]", (note the square brackets).
START and END are strings understood by `date`.
A single day may be specified by "[DATE]".

### CONTEXT
CONTEXT is an argument of the form "*USER*@*HOST*:*DIRECTORY*"
or "*USER*@*HOST*::*DIRECTORY*", where each field is optional.
"@" is used to specify user or host filters.
":" is used to specify a directory filter.
"::" may be used instead to exclude subdirectories.

### !
All three commands allow selecting from the 10 most recent entries
matching the filters by adding `!` to the command (ex. `h!`).
The selected command may be edited before it is executed.

The following command is also provided:

* **cd!** [*CONTEXT*] [*TIMESPEC*] [--] [*SEARCH*]
  * Select from recent working directories and optionally filter with pattern

## Examples

View all history
> h

View all commands matching the string `foo`
> h foo

View all commands run in this directory recursively
> dh

View all commands run in this directory only
> ldh

Select and edit from the most recent commands run in this directory only
> ldh!

View all commands starting with `echo`
> h ^echo

View all commands run yesterday containing `bar`
> h [yesterday] bar

View all commands run last week
> h [14 days ago..7 days ago]

View all commands run this month
> h [1 month ago..]

View all commands by user `baz`
> h baz@

View all commands run on hostname `host`
> h @host

View all commands containing the string `@host`
> h -- @host

View all commands run `/etc` recursively
> h :/etc

View all commands run in `/etc` only
> h ::/etc

View all commands run in the parent directory recursively
> h :..

Select from the most recent working directory locations
> cd!

Select from the most recent working directory locations running `vim`
> cd! ^vim

Select from the most recent working directory locations in `/etc`
> cd! :/etc

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

### Related Issues

_No response_

author: Talljack

title: Vault plugin on macOS with Apple silicon CPU's
body:
### Describe the bug

The Vault plugin is looking for vault's binary under`/usr/local/sbin/vault`. On Apple computers with M1/M2 processors if Vault was installed using Homebrew the location is `/opt/homebrew/bin/vault`
```
❯ source ~/.zshrc
❯ vault compgen:96: no such file or directory: /usr/local/sbin/vault
compgen:96: no such file or directory: /usr/local/sbin/vault
```
An easy workaround is:
```
❯ sudo mkdir /usr/local/sbin
❯ sudo ln -s /opt/homebrew/bin/vault /usr/local/sbin/vault
```

### Steps to reproduce

add vault to plugins in ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc

### Expected behavior

If /usr/local/sbin/vault does not exists warn about it, suggest the above workaround, fix

title: `grc` plugin does not source `grc.zsh` on Gentoo Linux
body:
### Describe the bug

The plugin uses the following paths for sourcing the `grc.zsh` file:

`plugins/grc/grc.plugin.zsh`:

```zsh
files=(
  /etc/grc.zsh               # default
  /usr/local/etc/grc.zsh     # homebrew darwin-x64
  /opt/homebrew/etc/grc.zsh  # homebrew darwin-arm64
)
```

The file `grc.zsh` is located at `/usr/share/grc/grc.zsh` for Gentoo Linux and thus not sourced.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Enable plugin `grc` on Gentoo Linux
2. Run command `ping 1.1`
3. Output is not colorized (and not called with grc)

### Expected behavior

% cat /etc/zshrc_Apple_Terminal
# Zsh support for Terminal.


# Working Directory
#
# Tell the terminal about the current working directory at each prompt.
#
# Terminal uses this to display the directory in the window title bar
# and tab bar, and for behaviors including creating a new terminal with
# the same working directory and restoring the working directory when
# restoring a terminal for Resume. See Terminal > Preferences for
# additional information.

if [ -z "$INSIDE_EMACS" ]; then

    update_terminal_cwd() {
	# Identify the directory using a "file:" scheme URL, including
	# the host name to disambiguate local vs. remote paths.

	# Percent-encode the pathname.
	local url_path=''
	{
	    # Use LC_CTYPE=C to process text byte-by-byte and
	    # LC_COLLATE=C to compare byte-for-byte. Ensure that
	    # LC_ALL and LANG are not set so they don't interfere.
	    local i ch hexch LC_CTYPE=C LC_COLLATE=C LC_ALL= LANG=
	    for ((i = 1; i <= ${#PWD}; ++i)); do
		ch="$PWD[i]"
		if [[ "$ch" =~ [/._~A-Za-z0-9-] ]]; then
		    url_path+="$ch"
		else
		    printf -v hexch "%02X" "'$ch"
		    url_path+="%$hexch"
		fi
	    done
	}

	printf '\e]7;%s\a' "file://$HOST$url_path"
    }

    # Register the function so it is called at each prompt.
    autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
    add-zsh-hook precmd update_terminal_cwd
fi


# Resume Support: Save/Restore Shell State
#
# Terminal assigns each terminal session a unique identifier and
# communicates it via the TERM_SESSION_ID environment variable so that
# programs running in a terminal can save/restore application-specific
# state when quitting and restarting Terminal with Resume enabled.
#
# The following code defines a shell save/restore mechanism. Users can
# add custom state by defining a `shell_session_save_user_state` function
# or an array of functions `shell_session_save_user_state_functions` that
# write restoration commands to the session state file at exit. The first
# argument of the function is the pathname of the session state file in
# which to store state. e.g., to save a variable:
#
#   shell_session_save_user_state() { echo MY_VAR="'$MY_VAR'" >> "$1"; }
#
# or:
#
#   save_my_var() { echo MY_VAR="'$MY_VAR'" >> "$1"; }
#   shell_session_save_user_state_functions+=(save_my_var)
#
# During shell startup the session file is executed and then deleted.
# You may save/restore arbitrarily complex/large state by writing it
# to some other file(s) and writing command(s) to the state file that
# restore that data. You should typically use the TERM_SESSION_ID
# as part of your file or directory names.
#
# The default behavior arranges to save and restore the shell command
# history independently for each restored terminal session. It also
# merges commands into the global history for new sessions. Because of
# this it is recommended that you set HISTSIZE and SAVEHIST to larger
# values.
#
# You may disable this behavior and share a single history by setting
# SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY to 0. The shell options INC_APPEND_HISTORY,
# INC_APPEND_HISTORY_TIME and SHARE_HISTORY are used to share new
# commands among running shells; therefore, if any of these is enabled,
# per-session history is disabled by default. You may explicitly enable
# it by setting SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY to 1.
#
# Note that this uses the precmd hook to enable per-session history the
# first time for each new session; if that doesn't run, the per-session
# history won't take effect until the first restore.
#
# The save/restore mechanism as a whole can be disabled by setting an
# environment variable (typically in `${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zshenv`):
#
#   SHELL_SESSIONS_DISABLE=1

if [ ${SHELL_SESSION_DID_INIT:-0} -eq 0 ] && [ -n "$TERM_SESSION_ID" ] && [ ${SHELL_SESSIONS_DISABLE:-0} -eq 0 ]; then

    # Do not perform this setup more than once.
    SHELL_SESSION_DID_INIT=1

    # Set up the session directory/file.
    SHELL_SESSION_DIR="${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zsh_sessions"
    SHELL_SESSION_FILE="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/$TERM_SESSION_ID.session"
    mkdir -m 700 -p "$SHELL_SESSION_DIR"

    #
    # Restore previous session state.
    #

    if [ -r "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE" ]; then
	. "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
	/bin/rm "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
    fi

    #
    # Note: Use absolute paths to invoke commands in the exit code and
    # anything else that runs after user startup files, because the
    # search path may have been modified.
    #

    #
    # Arrange for per-session shell command history.
    #

    shell_session_history_allowed() {
	# Return whether per-session history should be enabled.
	if [ -n "$HISTFILE" ]; then
	    # If this defaults to off, leave it unset so that we can
	    # check again later. If it defaults to on, make it stick.
	    local allowed=0
	    if [[ -o INC_APPEND_HISTORY ]] || [[ -o INC_APPEND_HISTORY_TIME ]] || [[ -o SHARE_HISTORY ]]; then
		allowed=${SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY:-0}
	    else
		allowed=${SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY:=1}
	    fi
	    if [ $allowed -eq 1 ]; then
		return 0
	    fi
	fi
	return 1
    }
    
    if [ ${SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY:-1} -eq 1 ]; then
	SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/$TERM_SESSION_ID.history"
	SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/$TERM_SESSION_ID.historynew"
	SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_SHARED="$HISTFILE"

	shell_session_history_enable() {
	    (umask 077; /usr/bin/touch "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW")
	    HISTFILE="$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW"
	    SHELL_SESSION_HISTORY=1
	}

	# If the session history already exists and isn't empty, start
	# using it now; otherwise, we'll use the shared history until
	# we've determined whether users have enabled/disabled this.
	if [ -s "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE" ]; then
	    fc -R "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE"
	    shell_session_history_enable
	else
	    # At the first prompt, check whether per-session history should
	    # be enabled. Delaying until after user scripts have run allows
	    # users to opt in or out. If this doesn't get executed (probably
	    # because a user script inadvertently removed the hook), we'll
	    # check at shell exit; that works, but doesn't start the per-
	    # session history until the first restore.

	    shell_session_history_check() {
		if [ ${SHELL_SESSION_DID_HISTORY_CHECK:-0} -eq 0 ]; then
		    SHELL_SESSION_DID_HISTORY_CHECK=1
		    shell_session_history_allowed && shell_session_history_enable
		    # Uninstall this check.
		    autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
		    add-zsh-hook -d precmd shell_session_history_check
		fi
	    }
	    autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
	    add-zsh-hook precmd shell_session_history_check
	fi

	shell_session_save_history() {
	    shell_session_history_enable
	    
	    # Save new history to an intermediate file so we can copy it.
	    fc -AI
	    
	    # If the session history doesn't exist yet, copy the shared history.
	    if [ -f "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_SHARED" ] && [ ! -s "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE" ]; then
		echo -ne '\n...copying shared history...' >&2
		(umask 077; /bin/cp "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_SHARED" "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE")
	    fi
	    
	    # Save new history to the per-session and shared files.
	    echo -ne '\n...saving history...' >&2
	    (umask 077; /bin/cat "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW" >> "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_SHARED")
	    (umask 077; /bin/cat "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW" >> "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE")
	    /bin/rm "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_NEW"
	    
	    # If there is a history file size limit, apply it to the files.
	    if [ -n "$SAVEHIST" ]; then
		echo -n 'truncating history files...' >&2
		fc -p "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE_SHARED" && fc -P
		fc -p "$SHELL_SESSION_HISTFILE" && fc -P
	    fi
	    echo -ne '\n...' >&2
	}
    fi

    #
    # Arrange to save session state when exiting the shell.
    #

    shell_session_save() {
	# Save the current state.
	if [ -n "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE" ]; then
	    echo -ne '\nSaving session...' >&2
	    (umask 077; echo 'echo Restored session: "$(/bin/date -r '$(/bin/date +%s)')"' >| "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE")
	    
	    # Call user-supplied hook functions to let them save state.
	    whence shell_session_save_user_state >/dev/null && shell_session_save_user_state "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
	    local f
	    for f in $shell_session_save_user_state_functions; do
		$f "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
	    done
	    
	    shell_session_history_allowed && shell_session_save_history
	    echo 'completed.' >&2
	fi
    }

    # Delete old session files. (Not more than once a day.)
    SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/_expiration_check_timestamp"
    shell_session_delete_expired() {
	if [ ! -e "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE" ] || [ -z "$(/usr/bin/find "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE" -mtime -1d)" ]; then
	    local expiration_lock_file="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/_expiration_lockfile"
	    if /usr/bin/shlock -f "$expiration_lock_file" -p $$; then
		echo -n 'Deleting expired sessions...' >&2
		local delete_count=$(/usr/bin/find "$SHELL_SESSION_DIR" -type f -mtime +2w -print -delete | /usr/bin/wc -l)
		[ "$delete_count" -gt 0 ] && echo $delete_count' completed.' >&2 || echo 'none found.' >&2
		(umask 077; /usr/bin/touch "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE")
		/bin/rm "$expiration_lock_file"
	    fi
	fi
    }
    
    # Update saved session state when exiting.
    shell_session_update() {
	shell_session_save && shell_session_delete_expired
    }
    autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
    add-zsh-hook zshexit shell_session_update
fi
```

            # If there is a history file size limit, apply it to the files.
            if [ -n "$SAVEHIST" ]; then

            fi
            echo -ne '\n...' >&2
        }
    fi

    #
    # Arrange to save session state when exiting the shell.
    #

    shell_session_save() {
        # Save the current state.
        if [ -n "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE" ]; then
            echo -ne '\nSaving session...' >&2
            (umask 077; echo 'echo Restored session: "$(/bin/date -r '$(/bin/date +%s)')"' >| "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE")
            
            # Call user-supplied hook functions to let them save state.
            whence shell_session_save_user_state >/dev/null && shell_session_save_user_state "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
            local f

                $f "$SHELL_SESSION_FILE"
            done
            
            shell_session_history_allowed && shell_session_save_history
            echo 'completed.' >&2
        fi
    }

    # Delete old session files. (Not more than once a day.)
    SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/_expiration_check_timestamp"
    shell_session_delete_expired() {
        if [ ! -e "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE" ] || [ -z "$(/usr/bin/find "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE" -mtime -1d)" ]; then
            local expiration_lock_file="$SHELL_SESSION_DIR/_expiration_lockfile"
            if /usr/bin/shlock -f "$expiration_lock_file" -p $$; then
                echo -n 'Deleting expired sessions...' >&2
                local delete_count=$(/usr/bin/find "$SHELL_SESSION_DIR" -type f -mtime +2w -print -delete | /usr/bin/wc -l)
                [ "$delete_count" -gt 0 ] && echo $delete_count' completed.' >&2 || echo 'none found.' >&2
                (umask 077; /usr/bin/touch "$SHELL_SESSION_TIMESTAMP_FILE")
                /bin/rm "$expiration_lock_file"
            fi
        fi
    }
    
    # Update saved session state when exiting.
    shell_session_update() {
        shell_session_save && shell_session_delete_expired
    }
    autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
    add-zsh-hook zshexit shell_session_update
fi

title: per-directory-history mode change message regenerates the prompt
body: ### Describe the bug With the `per-directory-mode` plugin active, toggling between modes causes the prompt to be recreated each time. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Enable `per-directory-history` plugin. 2. Enter `^G` to change modes. 3. Observe that the a `using global/local history` message is displayed, then a new prompt is creating, with the message persisting above it. ### Expected behavior I should be able to toggle between modes without the prompt regenerating and the lines advancing. In other words, the line showing at the top of my screen should remain at the top of my screen, regardless of how many times I toggle modes, until I actually run a command. ### Screenshots and recordings All I have done in the following screenshot is toggled the history mode with `^G` four times. Note that each time, a new prompt is created. ### OS / Linux distribution macOS Ventura 13.4.1 ### Zsh version 5.9 ### Terminal emulator iTerm2 ### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL None ### Additional context _No response_
