title: zsh-interactive-cd plugin always ignores hidden dirs
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

zsh-interactive-cd plugin

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

The plugin always ignores directories which start with a `.` and no configuration options exist to override this behaviour.

### Describe the proposed feature.

The plugin should have an env var option for opting in or out of having hidden directories being included in the list of suggestions.

Quick testing with this patch does seem to make hidden dirs visible but I assume that it is preferable to have an env var controlling this behaviour instead of code removal.

```diff
diff --git a/plugins/zsh-interactive-cd/zsh-interactive-cd.plugin.zsh b/plugins/zsh-interactive-cd/zsh-interactive-cd.plugin.zsh
index 0ae9d50a..6920fab0 100644
--- a/plugins/zsh-interactive-cd/zsh-interactive-cd.plugin.zsh
+++ b/plugins/zsh-interactive-cd/zsh-interactive-cd.plugin.zsh
@@ -24,9 +24,9 @@ __zic_matched_subdir_list() {
     fi
     find -L "$dir" -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d 2>/dev/null \
         | cut -b $(( ${length} + 2 ))- | command sed '/^$/d' | while read -r line; do
-      if [[ "${line[1]}" == "." ]]; then
-        continue
-      fi
+#      if [[ "${line[1]}" == "." ]]; then
+#        continue
+#      fi
       echo "$line"
     done
   else
@@ -40,9 +40,9 @@ __zic_matched_subdir_list() {
       find -L "$dir" -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -type d \
           2>/dev/null | cut -b $(( ${length} + 2 ))- | command sed '/^$/d' \
           | while read -r line; do
-        if [[ "${seg[1]}" != "." && "${line[1]}" == "." ]]; then
-          continue
-        fi
+#        if [[ "${seg[1]}" != "." && "${line[1]}" == "." ]]; then
+#          continue
+#        fi
         if [ "$zic_case_insensitive" = "true" ]; then
           if [[ "$line:u" == "$seg:u"* ]]; then
             echo "$line"
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only alternatives in this situation are to not use the plugin or to fork it and source my own instead of from omz.

### Additional context

No additional context.

### Related Issues

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/13131 already asks for one configuration option, it would save effort to add multiple of them at once.

title: Oh My Zsh interfering with latest iterm2 shell integration
body:
### Describe the bug

I noticed that on MacOS, shell integration would not install correctly, but did not show any errors when installing it. The reason I know it did not install correctly is that the icons used to copy command output and other functions do not show up (see picture)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/32c3b954-5d4d-431c-b967-a42cb0eb1299)

I am using `oh-my-zsh` with the `powerlevel10k` theme and have selectively removed each piece individually while restarting my shell. As soon as I comment out `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` in my `.zshrc` file, shell integration immediately starts to work. When I uncomment it, shell integration immediately breaks again. I have tried oh-my-zsh with and without the iterm2 plugin and with and without the `:omz:plugins:iterm2 shell-integration` option to zstyle.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run iTerm2 --> Install Shell Integration with oh-my-zsh.
2. Restart terminal window.
3. Run a command and see no icons from shell integration (broken).
4. Comment out `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` and restart the terminal window.
5. Run a command and see icons from shell integration (fixed).
6. Uncomment `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` and restart the terminal window.
7. Run a command and see no icons from shell integration (broken again).

### Expected behavior

The shell integration feature of iterm2 should work with oh-my-zsh.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

MacOS Sequoia 15.4.1

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (62afbde)

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2 Build 3.5.14

None

### Additional context

Problem exists with and without this set in my `.zshrc` file
```
ZSH_THEME="powerlevel10k/powerlevel10k"
```

Problem exists with this list of plugins, but also exists with a blank plugins list (`plugins=()`) in my `.zshrc` file
```
plugins=(
	git

	gh

title: Uninstalling Oh My Zsh deletes .zshrc
body:
### Describe the bug

I have a .zshrc I keep in a github repo. And before you ask why - it's for good reasons, like the one below.

I share my shell config and aliases among all my computers. I've been using it for the last couple of years, gradually accumulating useful stuff. I use the same setup on many computers - seven computers currently, and many, many others previously.

I have installed Oh My Zsh the very first time I started using zsh several years ago.

Today I tried uninstalling Oh My Zsh.

You can see where this is going; it all went swimmingly well. Because as you might have guessed, I _do not_ have on any of my current computers the backup the Oh My Zsh installer created several years and multiple computers ago.

And frankly, _even_ if I had that backup from several years ago... does anyone really, truly believes restoring it and losing all the config changes since then would be the right thing to do?

And that folks is why I keep my stuff in git.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install Oh My Zsh.
2. Commit your .zshrc to github repo.
3. Change job, return old computer, and get new computer.
4. Clone the github repo.
5. Observe everything is configured as you expect.
6. Uninstall Oh My Zsh.
7. Observe your .zshrc is obliterated.

### Expected behavior

Do not delete .zshrc, expecting a backup that was created several years and multiple computers ago to exist or if it does, to be relevant and useful.

Windows 11 / WSL / Ubuntu 24, OS X, Raspbian

title: Color overflow in the git status in the agnoster theme in VSCode
body:
### Describe the bug

In a very specific scenario when using the integrated terminal in VSCode, the part in the prompt to display the git status will have a color overflow all the way to the right end of the line. 
 
It looks like the following:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1e8cc9bb-9921-4601-b7e9-07ea1201068f)

Resizing the terminal makes it normal

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f4e4a21e-346f-4adc-9078-7624e8cd9cd1)

I don't whether this is a VSCode bug or Oh My Zsh bug.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open up the integrated terminal in VSCode.
2. Moving the integrated terminal as one of the panels, instead of the default location at the bottom. 
3. Set the width of the terminal "kinda narrow", such that the pwd directory is long enough.
4. Execute some commands that show things.
5. Open up something in VSCode (e.g., chat window from the command palette), it pushes the terminal to a certain degree that the prompt will span multiple lines. Now the color from the git status bar will overflow.

### Expected behavior

author: scaomath

title: SSH plugin completion not cleaning line endings
body:
### Describe the bug

If using the `ssh` plugin to provide completions based on `~/.ssh/config`, any definition that contains CRLF will be reported as is in the completion. 




### Steps to reproduce

1. Define an entry in `~/.ssh/config`, making sure the `Host somehost` part has a CRLF ending.
2. Validate (eg: `file -k .ssh/config` should print `.ssh/config: ASCII text, with CRLF line terminators`).
3. In zsh terminal, write `ssh some[TAB]` ("normally" expecting `ssh somehost` to be completed).
4. Observe that instead, the completion is `ssh somehost$'\r'`

### Expected behavior

The plugin should handle non-Unix file endings properly. This is a common issue when using similar ssh configurations across windows/unix machines. 

Temporary workaround is to run `dos2unix` on the config, but it would be nicer if this was handled transparently.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Windows 10 and Ubuntu 22

### Zsh version

5.8.1

### Terminal emulator

Gnome Terminal

None

### Additional context

_No response_

title: VSCode + Cursor: zsh: command not found: q
body:
### Describe the bug

**Agent Mode: Terminal hangs due to `zsh: command not found: q` – possibly related to oh-my-zsh**

I'm encountering an issue in Cursor (which runs on VSCode) where the terminal hangs indefinitely after executing a command. It seems the system fails to detect that the previous command has completed.

When skipping the stalled command (by clicking “Skip” in the Cursor chat window), the following message appears:

```
zsh: command not found: q
```

It looks like something is automatically entering `q` after each CLI command execution. This behavior has been discussed on the Cursor Forum and may be related to `oh-my-zsh`.

**Relevant threads:**

author: Jont828

title: Automatically Install plugins
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

The problem is, I would need to clone the plugins to the required directory and then add them to the list of plugins. This feels like too much work, considering that oh-my-zsh is a plugin manager. It should be able to handle automatic installation if missing. This way, I can use my `.zshrc` anywhere and not have to worry about writing a script to install plugins for me.

### Describe the proposed feature.

I can't think of how this should work specifically but my suggestion would be to have a list of plugins git location. e.g,
```bash
plugins=(
     "zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions"
)
```
So if the plugin doesn't already exist, it should clone the repo with the information passed. It should still be able to take just the name, in case it already exists. 

And then 2 new commands can be added to sync (install all missing plugin) and update (to update plugins). 
If a plugin is not found at startup, users can be prompted to choose whether they want to install the missing plugins or ignore them. There should also be a config to always ignore, always ask, or always update. 

Pinning a plugin to a particular branch or commit would be nice to have, although, I can't really think of how that would be added.


I hope I am making sense.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I have a script to install plugins. While this is not bad, I still always have to edit the script and when I install a new plugin. I use different PC for different things and I like to be able to reproduce my environment anywhere.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: kubectl plugin: update namespace of current context
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

kubectl plugin

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

The kubectl plugin is awesome, but if you're not working in the `default` namespace you always have to supply the `-n` flag with each namespaced command. You can of course edit the current context to change the default namespace, but that's also a long command.

### Describe the proposed feature.

New aliases:

```sh
# update default ns of the current context
alias kcsetns="kcsc --current --namespace"

# get default ns of current context
alias kcgetns="k config view --minify -o 'jsonpath={.contexts[0].context.namespace}'"
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Writing out the aforementioned commands. Or in my case I simply added the aliases to my own custom config in `~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/custom.zsh`

title: Filter for verified PRs in `omz pr test`
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

In `omz pr test`, we allow the user to check out any PR submitted to our repository.

This is a potential issue because essentially the user is going to be running untrusted code.

We can work around that by only allowing to directly test PRs that have been assigned the `testers needed` label.

Policy-wise, we should also make some kind of automated or semi-automated review of PRs with such labels, to lower the chance of misuse.

### Describe the proposed feature.

Check with GitHub API if the PR being checked-out has the `testers needed` label.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Provide a prompt to the user explaining that they are about to check out unverified, untrusted code. It's not that final of a solution because we can't ensure the user will understand the consequences of doing so.

### Additional context

W2M1-09

### Related Issues

_No response_

title: Comment for `\C-x\C-e` Key Code Is Half Correct and Could Be Clarified
body:
### Describe the bug

Sorry, this isn't a bug per se. But, I couldn't think of a better category to submit under. 

The [comment for the `\C-x\C-e` keybinding](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/2423b7a12dc4624a2d8a7c58be4ac75cb82fd8c7/lib/key-bindings.zsh#L120) states:

> Edit the current command line in $EDITOR

But, it actually attempts to open `$VISUAL` first and then falls back to `$EDITOR`. I verified this both on macOS and Ubuntu.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Set the `$VISUAL` environment variable to an editor (e.g., `vscode --wait`).
2. Set the `$EDITOR` environment variable to another editor (e.g., `vim`).
3. Go to the terminal and type out a command.
4. Enter <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>X</kbd> > <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>E</kbd>.
5. Observe that the command line is opened in `$VISUAL`.

### Expected behavior

The comment should state the correct behavior of `$VISUAL` being invoked, with `$EDITOR` as a fallback. Maybe as a bonus the keybinding could be described as well? Something like:

> [Ctrl-x] > [Ctrl-e] - Edit the current command line in $VISUAL or $EDITOR if the former is unset

author: jbenner-radham

title: 1password plugin ssh login with password and plugin.
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

1password plugin

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

I need to login to an ssh server that disallows ssh-keys for security reasons. I must use a password and a time-based one time password (totp). I wanted to use the 1password plugin to do that but couldn't.

### Describe the proposed feature.

My proposed solution (also described in <https://github.com/1Password/shell-plugins/issues/348>) is actually working, but I'm not sure how best to move forward. I know I could fork the project but I need some minor help ensuring that it always works. Furthermore, my solution requires a forked version of sshpass because of the need for a totp. The forked version of sshpass that handles totp is here: <https://github.com/dora38/sshpass>.

I also added a new "Website" in 1password with the label "ssh" that has the ssh url. Finally, my code is a modification of the ohmyzsh 1password plugin. I modified this file <https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/1password/opswd>.

The following code works for me and I'd love for it to be integrated somehow into ohmyzsh since I took the time to write this and most of the underlying code is already in ohmyzsh. 

Finally, this works if there is a totp, I didn't check to see if it works properly if there isn't. It probably still needs some work. It would also be great if the plugin could be written in such as way that the `.ssh/config` file could include the `ProxyCommand` so that other config variables get used.

I'd love to hear about how to incorporate this plugin. I am copying @carlosala because he made the last edit of the 1password plugin.

```
#autoload

# opsssh requires sshpass from (https://github.com/dora38/sshpass) 
# This assumes there is a totp and I haven't modified it for cases where it's not needed.

function opssh() {
  if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "Usage: opssh <service>"
    return 1
  fi

  local service=$1

  # If not logged in, print error and return
  op user list > /dev/null || return

  local host
  # Get the host
  if ! host=$(op item get "$service" --format json | jq -r '.urls[] | select(.label=="ssh").href' 2>/dev/null); then
    echo "error: could not obtain host for $service"
    return 1
  fi

  local username
  # Get the username
  if ! username=$(op item get "$service" --fields username 2>/dev/null); then
    echo "error: could not obtain username for $service"
    return 1
  fi

  local password
  # Get the password
  if ! password=$(op item get "$service" --reveal --fields password 2>/dev/null); then
    echo "error: could not obtain password for $service"
    return 1
  fi

  # If there's a one time password, Get that
  local totp
  if ! totp=$(op item get --otp "$service" 2>/dev/null) && [[ -n "$totp" ]]; then
    echo "error: could not obtain one time password (totp) for $service"
    return 1
  fi

  # login with sshpass from (https://github.com/dora38/sshpass)
  # add -v for more details if desired
  sshpass -p "$password" -o "$totp" "ssh" $username@$host
}

opssh "$@"
```


### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

Rails plugin

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

New Rails applications can have a multiple database system, that means that Rails commands like `rails db:rollback` need to specify the database such as `rails db:rollback:primary`. There are 3 main databases that we could add commands for, which are `primary`, `cache`, `cable` and `queue`.

### Describe the proposed feature.

Add the alias to the Rails Plugin

```zsh
alias rdccb='rails db:create:cable'
alias rdcch='db:create:cache'
alias rdcp='rails db:create:primary'
alias rdcq='rails db:create:queue'

alias rddcb='rails db:drop:cable'
alias rddch='rails db:drop:cache'
alias rddp='rails db:drop:primary'
alias rddq='rails db:drop:queue'

alias rdmcb='rails db:migrate:cable'
alias rdmch='rails db:migrate:cache'
alias rdmp='rails db:migrate:primary'
alias rdmq='rails db:migrate:queue'

alias rdmdcb='rails db:migrate:down:cable'
alias rdmdch='rails db:migrate:down:cache'
alias rdmdp='rails db:migrate:down:primary'
alias rdmdq='rails db:migrate:down:queue'

alias rdmrcb='rails db:migrate:redo:cable'
alias rdmrch='rails db:migrate:redo:cache'
alias rdmrp='rails db:migrate:redo:primary'
alias rdmrq='rails db:migrate:redo:queue'

alias rdmscb='rails db:migrate:status:cable'
alias rdmsch='rails db:migrate:status:cache'
alias rdmsp='rails db:migrate:status:primary'
alias rdmsq='rails db:migrate:status:queue'

alias rdmucb='rails db:migrate:up:cable'
alias rdmuch='rails db:migrate:up:cache'
alias rdmup='rails db:migrate:up:primary'
alias rdmuq='rails db:migrate:up:queue'

alias  rdrcb='rails db:rollback:cable'
alias  rdrch='rails db:rollback:cache'
alias  rdrp='rails db:rollback:primary'
alias  rdrq='rails db:rollback:queue'

alias rdrscb='rails db:reset:cable'
alias rdrsch='rails db:reset:cache'
alias rdrsp='rails db:reset:primary'
alias rdrsq='rails db:reset:queue'

alias rdslcb='rails db:schema:load:cable'
alias rdslch='rails db:schema:load:cache'
alias rdslp='rails db:schema:load:primary'
alias rdslq='rails db:schema:load:queue'
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Maybe the proposed alias are to many. So instead of adding all of them, we could add only the ones that require the database to be specified, like for example `rails db:rollback`. Or add alias only for the primary database, since probably thats the most used one.

author: jayakrishnancn

title: brew plugin should eval shellenv even if brew is already on the path
body:
### Describe the bug

Currently, the brew plugin [only does](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/9e23925b8581d22033f07f1983587412d3761494/plugins/brew/brew.plugin.zsh#L19) `eval "$(brew shellenv)"` if `brew` is not already on the path. I believe this is suboptimal and counter to the intent of the Homebrew project, at least on macOS (I haven't used Homebrew on any other OSes, so I can't speak to that).

The default Homebrew install on macOS adds an `/etc/paths.d/homebrew` file, with the following content (this is on Apple Silicon, anyway – the default install location is different on Intel):

```
/opt/homebrew/bin
```

This causes `path_helper` to add this directory to the `PATH`, _after_ the default entries (`/usr/local/bin`, `/usr/bin`, `/bin` etc.) when it is run from `/etc/zprofile`. I believe the intent here is to provide a decent fallback for users that don't do any further configuration of their own shell. Note, though, that Homebrew also [recommends](https://docs.brew.sh/Installation#post-installation-steps) (for vanilla Zsh) adding the following to your `~/.zshrc`:

```
eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
```

This is unguarded, i.e. it runs even when `brew` is already on the path, and they have every reason to expect it would be, as outlined above.

Here's the output of `shellenv` on my system:

```
export HOMEBREW_PREFIX="/opt/homebrew";
export HOMEBREW_CELLAR="/opt/homebrew/Cellar";
export HOMEBREW_REPOSITORY="/opt/homebrew";
fpath[1,0]="/opt/homebrew/share/zsh/site-functions";
PATH="/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/System/Cryptexes/App/usr/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:/var/run/com.apple.security.cryptexd/codex.system/bootstrap/usr/local/bin:/var/run/com.apple.security.cryptexd/codex.system/bootstrap/usr/bin:/var/run/com.apple.security.cryptexd/codex.system/bootstrap/usr/appleinternal/bin"; export PATH;
[ -z "${MANPATH-}" ] || export MANPATH=":${MANPATH#:}";
export INFOPATH="/opt/homebrew/share/info:${INFOPATH:-}";
```

As you can see, part of what `shellenv` does is it produces a _reordered_ path, with `/opt/homebrew/bin` at the start. This gives commands installed with Homebrew priority over those shipped by macOS, which is usually what users would want, since Homebrew usually provides newer versions.

But the brew plugin for Oh My Zsh doesn't do this. It skips `brew shellenv` because `brew` is already on the path, so users are left with the default `path_helper` ordering, where system commands run instead of the ones they explicitly install with Homebrew. This is a bad experience.

I believe the solution is simple: The plugin should always run `brew shellenv` and eval the output, as long as `brew` is installed. Also, you'll see above, that also handles setting `HOMEBREW_PREFIX` and adding Homebrew's shell completion functions, so there would be no need to do those steps in the plugin if it didn't skip `brew shellenv`.

### Steps to reproduce

1. [Install Homebrew](https://brew.sh/) on macOS:
   ```
   /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
   ```

2. In a new shell, install the git formula:
   ```
   brew install git
   ```

3. Add the brew plugin to Oh My Zsh.
4. In a new shell, check which git runs:
   ```
   git --version
   ```

   You'll see that it is the older version from macOS:
   ```
   git version 2.39.5 (Apple Git-154)
   ```

5. Check the path.
   ```
   echo $PATH
   ```

   You'll see that the Homebrew bin directory is near the end.

### Expected behavior

The newer version of Git from Homebrew runs:

```
git version 2.51.0
```

The Homebrew bin directory appears near the start of the path, ahead of `/usr/bin` and `/bin`.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

macOS 15.6

### Zsh version

5.9

iTerm2

author: davesteinberg

title: Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/discussions/13301
Originally posted by **rgoldberg** September 13, 2025 Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly using the latest (and versions since at least 1.5 years ago) Oh My Zsh on zsh 5.9 included with macOS 15.6.1 (& with earlier macOS versions) or installed from Homebrew core (I presume that all distributions of zsh 5.9, as well as older versions of zsh, suffer from the issue). See . I don't know the exact requirements for the failure, but it seems like, if you try to complete after a single `-`, and if a short & long option designate the same option (it might occur for all shorts & longs being distinct, or for multiple shorts or multiple longs designating the same option, etc.), then the display of the candidates below the command line is visually messed up, either way each long, each short, and each description on its own line, repeated multiple times (I've seen 3 repeats, but maybe the repeat count differs under circumstances). Candidates seem to be displayed correctly if you try to complete after `--`. The problem goes away if you disable Oh My Zsh or all Oh My Zsh matchers for completions, e.g. via: ```shell zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list '' ``` I've attached a completion script that suffers from this problem: [_mas.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22309822/_mas.txt) (I had to append `.txt` to attach it). Is there any way that existing completion scripts can be made compatible with Oh My Zsh fuzzy matching? If so, how? Either way, is there a GitHub issue that tracks fixing this? Oh My Zsh should be robust enough to not mess up display of valid zsh completion candidates. If the issue cannot be fixed, then should fuzzy matching be removed? Either way, should fuzzy matching be disabled by default until the problem is fixed?

author: MrSimmmons

title: Homebrew command-not-found code has changed
body:
### Describe the bug

Homebrew moved their `command-not-found` into the core repository, and correspondingly have new install instructions: https://docs.brew.sh/Command-Not-Found#install
```zsh
HOMEBREW_COMMAND_NOT_FOUND_HANDLER="$(brew --repository)/Library/Homebrew/command-not-found/handler.sh"
if [ -f "$HOMEBREW_COMMAND_NOT_FOUND_HANDLER" ]; then
  source "$HOMEBREW_COMMAND_NOT_FOUND_HANDLER";
fi
```
These are not currently used by the `command-not-found` plugin.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Update Homebrew to latest version
2. Enable `command-not-found` plugin.
3. `omz reload` or open new shell.
4. The following warning is printed:
```
Warning: Homebrew's command-not-found integration is out of date.
Please update your shell configuration to the new instructions printed by:
  brew command-not-found-init
```

### Expected behavior

No warning is printed and `command-not-found` functions properly.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

macOS 15.7

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (58cba61)

Terminal.app

None

### Additional context

This change was introduced in https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/pull/20730

title: uninstall.sh drop all my settings and tokens stored in .zshrc
body:
### Describe the bug

My carelessness and false expectations led to the funniest situation in the last 10 years. (It would have been tragic if I didn’t have a backup.)

After uninstall.sh finished running, I started reading through its log lines. I see the line:
`Found ~/.zshrc -- Renaming to /Users/a0s/.zshrc.omz-uninstalled-2025-09-23_12-41-12`
and I think, great, I can delete it, since it’s just a backup that includes source oh-my-zsh.sh. So I run rm (yes, I rushed). Then I read:
`Found /Users/a0s/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh -- Restoring to ~/.zshrc`
and start feeling a bit confusing, because it’s been a very long time since I first installed oh-my-zsh. And then I open `~/.zshrc` and see an almost empty file. All my configs and tokens have gone to Valhalla. Thanks for the awesome uninstaller! :) 

I fully realize that in the current situation I’m at fault, but overwriting the current config with a file that’s many, many years old also seems unreasonable.

### Steps to reproduce

### Expected behavior

It should try to find the line with "source oh-my-zsh.sh" and try to remove it.

Are you sure you want to remove Oh My Zsh? [y/N] Y

author: zerogog

title: Transient Prompt - appears to require enter twice for first command in new shell
body:
### Describe the bug

First off, just wanted to say LOVE Oh-My-Posh.

I've been setting up my new prompt and I was testing out the transient prompt, but I'm seeing some very weird behavior. when I start a new terminal window or start a new zsh shell within the terminal, the first command when I hit enter doesn't work properly, instead of running the `ls` command it seems to swap to the transient prompt and I need to hit enter a second time in order to get the command to work. After that things work fine, unless I start a new shell within the same terminal then again the first command doesn't work properly.

Unfortunately I shift shells quite a bit so this is pretty disruptive to me, and was hoping for a fix or perhaps someone can point out what I am doing wrong.

### Steps to reproduce

Use the following simple config which reproduces the issue:
```json
{
    "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/main/themes/schema.json",
    "transient_prompt": {},
    "blocks": [
        {
            "type": "prompt",
            "alignment": "left",
            "segments": [
                {
                    "template": ">",
                    "type": "text",
                    "style": "plain"
                }
            ]
        }
    ],
    "version": 3
}
```

I've also recorded a video which shows the issues with keystrokes displayed attached below.

Steps:
1. Load the prompt config and start a new terminal (i'm using iterm2 3.6.2)
2. as soon as the terminal opens and loads the primary prompt, type `ls` and enter
3. notice how it doesn't actually run the command but switches to the transient prompt
4. hit enter again and it will run normally after that
5. if you start `zsh` again the issue reappears.


### Expected behavior

I would expect the first prompt to `enter` just like normal and then display the transient prompt.

### Screenshots and recordings

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a190e60c-67bb-4645-8473-0aa7134d307f)

### OS / Linux distribution

OSX Tahoe

### Zsh version

5.9

zsh> oh-my-posh version 26.25.0

iterm 3.6.2

author: Vonarian

title: Add Pull Request (PR) Template
body:
Currently, the repository does not have a Pull Request (PR) template. This makes it harder for contributors to provide consistent information when opening PRs.

Proposed Solution:
Add a PR template file at .github/pull_request_template.md.

The template should include:
Description of changes
Related issues (Fixes #issue)
Screenshots (if applicable)
Type of change (Bug fix, Feature, Enhancement, Documentation)
Checklist (self-review, code style, documentation update)

Benefits:
Ensures all PRs have consistent structure.
Helps maintainers review PRs faster.
Improves contributor experience and professionalism of the project.

title: NVM not working if the NVM_SOURCE and  NVM_DIR are different
body:
### Describe the bug

Hi everyone,

I've been trying to set up nvm on my Arch Linux system using the package available in the [official repository](https://archlinux.org/packages/extra/any/nvm/). However, it seems that nvm is not being loaded correctly. When the necessary exports are configured properly, everything works as expected.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Set NVM_SOURCE to the installation directory

    example:
    ```
    export NVM_SOURCE="/usr/share/nvm/nvm.sh"

    ``` 
2. Set NVM_SOURCE to the home directory

    example:
    ```
    export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"

    ``` 

NVM will not load automatically

### Expected behavior

NVM should work as expected

### OS / Linux distribution

Arch Linux

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master

### Terminal emulator

Konsole

Looking at the source I saw that the NVM_SOURCE is not being used, only NVM_DIR. I've fixed this in [this](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/13397) PR

title: zcompdump files should prrobably be stored in $ZSH_CACHE_DIR
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

oh-my-zsh.sh defines a $ZSH_CACHE_DIR that uses XDG_CACHE_HOME (awesome)

```
# Set ZSH_CACHE_DIR to the path where cache files should be created
# or else we will use the default cache/
if [[ -z "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR" ]]; then
  ZSH_CACHE_DIR="$ZSH/cache"
fi

# Make sure $ZSH_CACHE_DIR is writable, otherwise use a directory in $HOME
if [[ ! -w "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR" ]]; then
  ZSH_CACHE_DIR="${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/oh-my-zsh"
fi
```

But then later in this file this is not used by zcompdump (which feels like it fits the definition of cache file)

```
# Save the location of the current completion dump file.
if [[ -z "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" ]]; then
  ZSH_COMPDUMP="${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zcompdump-${SHORT_HOST}-${ZSH_VERSION}"
fi
```

### Describe the proposed feature.

Cleaning up by home directory

author: JSchoreels

title: zsh-autosuggestions plugin should give suggestions based on current directory
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

zsh-autosuggestions

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

## Problem Description

The current autosuggestion feature, which relies on command input history, uses a global history log.

This approach leads to non-optimal suggestions, especially when switching between different projects or directories. Commands with similar initial characters often require vastly different arguments depending on the specific folder/project context.

## Examples of the issue

In one project directory, a user might use` git push origin main`, but in another project, they might use `git push origin master`.

Similarly, one project might require `nvm use 18.10.0`, while another needs `nvm use 22.0.0`.

When developing both projects concurrently, the autosuggestion frequently suggests the last globally-used command, which is often irrelevant, confusing, and requires manual correction, thereby reducing the plugin's effectiveness.

## Describe the proposed feature.

Implementing a mechanism to provide command suggestions based on the input history specific to the current directory (or project root) would solve this problem.

This directory-based/contextual history would significantly improve the accuracy and utility of the autosuggestion feature, allowing the plugin to better serve users working on multiple distinct projects.

author: Ray-Zhang-33

title: alias-finder: BSD grep incompatibility with '{0,1} pattern causes errors on macOS
body: ### Describe the bug The `alias-finder` plugin generates `grep: invalid repetition count(s)` errors on macOS when the `autoload` option is enabled. This occurs on EVERY command execution due to BSD grep's incompatibility with the `'{0,1}` regex pattern. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Enable alias-finder plugin in `~/.zshrc`: ```bash plugins=(alias-finder) zstyle ':omz:plugins:alias-finder' autoload yes 2. Reload shell: source ~/.zshrc 3. Run any command (e.g., ls, cd /tmp, echo hello) 4. Observe error appearing before command output: grep: invalid repetition count(s) ### Expected behavior Commands should execute without grep errors. ### Screenshots and recordings ### OS / Linux distribution macOS 15.1 (Sequoia) - affects all macOS versions ### Zsh version zsh 5.9 (arm64-apple-darwin24.0) ### Terminal emulator iTerm2 / Terminal.app (affects all terminal emulators) ### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL None ### Additional context ### Root cause: The plugin uses this pattern in line 2 of alias-finder.plugin.zsh: `wordEnd="'{0,1}$"` Later combined into: ``` finder="'{0,1}$cmd$wordEnd" alias | grep -E "=$finder" ``` BSD grep 2.6.0 (shipped with macOS since 2010) cannot parse '{0,1} - the leading single quote confuses its regex parser, causing it to reject the repetition count {0,1}. ### Workaround: Install GNU grep and ensure it's in PATH before Oh My Zsh loads: ``` # Install GNU grep brew install grep # Add to ~/.zshrc BEFORE sourcing oh-my-zsh.sh export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/grep/libexec/gnubin:$PATH" ``` ### Proposed solutions: 1. Recommended: Use BSD-compatible regex pattern: `wordEnd="'\\?$"` # Use \? for zero-or-one (equivalent to {0,1}) 3. Alternative: Document GNU grep requirement in README with installation instructions 4. Existing pattern: Already checks for rg (ripgrep) - could prioritize it over grep when available ### Distinction from existing issues: This issue is distinct from: - #13016 (1-character command errors) - fixed via early return - #13039 (256+ character errors) - fixed via ripgrep preference Those fixes addressed edge cases but didn't resolve the fundamental BSD grep incompatibility with the wordEnd pattern that affects all macOS users running system grep. The '{0,1} pattern fails universally on BSD grep regardless of command length, making this a more fundamental compatibility issue. ### Impact: - Affects all macOS users with default system grep - Appears on every command when autoload is enabled - Has existed since plugin creation (BSD grep unchanged since 2010) ### grep version: grep (BSD grep, GNU compatible) 2.6.0-FreeBSD

title: Auto-Fix corrupted history (Suggestion/Solution)
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

_No response_

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

Hello, 
I had corrupted history for a few times now and I found that manual fix is kinda time consuming so I decided to implement auto-fix, by adding a simple script that checks if the corruption is present, and triggers the fix if needed. If not needed, the script stays silent. I just added it into one custom config file. Works great for me, maybe someone else could use it.

### Describe the proposed feature.

```
# Fix corrupted history if present
_fix_zsh_history() {
    local hist="${HISTFILE:-$HOME/.zsh_history}"

    if [[ -n "$_ZSH_FIX_ATTEMPTED" ]]; then
        unset _ZSH_FIX_ATTEMPTED
        return
    fi

    [[ ! -f "$hist" ]] && return

    local zsh_complaint
    zsh_complaint=$(fc -R "$hist" 2>&1 >/dev/null)

    if [[ "$zsh_complaint" == *"corrupt"* ]]; then
        echo -e "\n\033[0;31m[!] Zsh corrupted history detected.\033[0m"
        echo -e "\033[0;33m[+] Scrubbing and restarting shell...\033[0m"

        local backup="${hist}_bad_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)"
        local temp_hist="${hist}.tmp"

        command mv "$hist" "$backup"
        command strings "$backup" > "$temp_hist" 2>/dev/null

        if [[ -s "$temp_hist" ]]; then
            command mv "$temp_hist" "$hist"
        else
            touch "$hist"
        fi

        export _ZSH_FIX_ATTEMPTED=1
        exec zsh
    fi
}

# Run the fix script
_fix_zsh_history
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

author: mcornella

title: extract plugin: Add unar as fallback for RAR files when unrar is not available
body:
## Problem

The extract plugin hardcodes `unrar` for RAR extraction:

```zsh
(*.rar) unrar x -ad "$full_path" ;;
```

However, `unrar` has been removed from Homebrew due to licensing issues ([Homebrew Discussion #285](https://github.com/orgs/Homebrew/discussions/285)).

## Proposed Solution

Add `unar` as a fallback when `unrar` is not available:

```zsh
(*.rar)
  if (( $+commands[unrar] )); then
    unrar x -ad "$full_path"
  elif (( $+commands[unar] )); then
    unar -d "$full_path"
  else
    echo "extract: cannot extract RAR files - install unar or unrar"
    return 1
  fi ;;
```

## Context

- `unar` is available via Homebrew: `brew install unar`
- It handles RAR files (and 30+ other formats) and is actively maintained

## Environment

- macOS (Homebrew)
- ohmyzsh extract plugin via antidote

author: boyzoid

title: The inclusion of Grok as a search engine is concerning given recent CSAM news and does not reflect well on ohmyzsh
body:
Grok is at current a [notable source of CSAM and nonconsensual pornography](https://time.com/7344858/grok-deepfake-crisis-explained/) to the point where [Wired has called for its removal from app stores](https://www.wired.com/story/x-grok-app-store-nudify-csam-apple-google-content-moderation/).

To [quote from CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2026/01/08/tech/elon-musk-xai-digital-undressing):

> Researchers at AI Forensics, a European non-profit that investigates algorithms, analyzed over 20,000 random images generated by Grok and 50,000 user requests between December 25 and January 1.
> 
> The researchers found “a high prevalence of terms including ‘her’ ‘put’/’remove,’ ‘bikini,’ and ‘clothing.’” More than half of the images generated of people, or 53%, “contained individuals in minimal attire such as underwear or bikinis, of which 81% were individuals presenting as women,” the researchers found. Notably, 2% of images depicted people appearing to be 18 years old or younger, the researchers found.
> 
> AI Forensics also found that in some cases, users requested minors be put in erotic positions and that sexual fluids be depicted on their bodies. Grok complied with those requests, according to AI Forensics.

While xAI has responded that they are looking into safeguards,

> ...AI safety experts said the platform ignored months of warnings that such abuse was imminent.
> 
> “In August, we warned that xAI’s image generation was essentially a nudification tool ‌waiting to be weaponised,” said Tyler Johnston, executive director of AI watchdog group The Midas Project. “That’s basically what’s played out.”

\- [EU flags ‘appalling’ child-like deepfakes generated by X’s Grok AI](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/1/5/eu-flags-appalling-child-like-deepfakes-generated-via-xs-grok-ai)

xAI has also [placed the blame on users rather than their own technology](https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2026/01/x-blames-users-for-grok-generated-csam-no-fixes-announced/).

While [AI-generated nonconsensual pornography is a growing problem beyond the scope of just Grok](https://www.engadget.com/ai/reports-indicate-a-massive-uptick-in-ai-generated-csam-throughout-the-internet-154937671.html), Grok is a very public and politicized offender on this front to the point of [many investigations by watchdog groups](https://www.forbes.com/sites/emmawoollacott/2026/01/08/watchdogs-around-the-world-probing-grok-over-csam-accusations/).

I would highly recommend removing Grok as a search engine in light of this news.

title: [BUG] trace trap crash on launch after Oh My Zsh update - cursor focus moves to prompt line
body:
### Describe the bug

### Preflight Checklist

- [x] I have searched existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet
- [x] This is a single bug report (please file separate reports for different bugs)
- [x] I am using the latest version of Claude Code

### What's Wrong?

After updating Oh My Zsh (commit `e0766905519fbc7982a590a195ee7c3e8bb594e8` from 2026-01-20), Claude Code crashes immediately on launch with a `trace trap` error.

The TUI briefly appears showing the welcome screen, but the cursor focus incorrectly moves from the input prompt (`> Try "edit annotations-actions.ts to..."`) down to the zsh prompt line (`books git:(toolkit-ui)`), and then the process terminates with:
```
[1]  34847 trace trap  command claude
```

### Environment

- **Claude Code version:** 2.1.12
- **macOS:** 26.2 (Build 25C56)
- **Chip:** Apple M2
- **Node.js:** v22.19.0 (via nvm)
- **Shell:** zsh with Oh My Zsh
- **Terminal:** iTerm2 with shell integration enabled
- **Oh My Zsh commit:** e0766905519fbc7982a590a195ee7c3e8bb594e8 (2026-01-20)

### Oh My Zsh Configuration

Theme: `robbyrussell`

Plugins:
- git
- macos
- common-aliases
- pod
- sudo
- yarn
- zsh-autosuggestions
- zsh-syntax-highlighting

Also using:
- iTerm2 Shell Integration (`~/.iterm2_shell_integration.zsh`)
- zsh-syntax-highlighting from Homebrew

### What I've Tried

1. Setting `ITERM_SHELL_INTEGRATION_INSTALLED=No` before launching - **did not help**
2. Disabling `ZSH_AUTOSUGGEST` - **did not help**
3. Using a clean environment with `env -i` - **not tested yet**

### What Should Happen

Claude Code should launch normally and maintain cursor focus in its TUI input area.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Have Oh My Zsh installed with latest update (2026-01-20)
2. Have iTerm2 shell integration enabled
3. Run `claude` in terminal
4. Observe: TUI appears briefly, cursor moves to wrong line, process crashes with trace trap

### Is this a regression?

Yes, this worked before the Oh My Zsh update on 2026-01-20.

### Related Issues

- #19026 - Similar trace trap issue on macOS
- #1872 - Oh My Zsh compatibility issues with shell snapshots

### Additional Information

The issue seems related to how Claude Code's TUI interacts with Oh My Zsh's prompt rendering or iTerm2's shell integration escape sequences.

### Steps to reproduce

1. open claude-code and start typing
2. cursor will start in claude-code cli 
3. focus moves to the oh-my-zsh prompt (can not type anymore in claude-code)

### Expected behavior

1. open claude-code and start typing
2. cursor will start in claude-code cli 
3. focus it's trap in claude-code prompt

### Screenshots and recordings

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7a857c74-105c-4744-b1cf-f5125ffdfb00

**macOS:** 26.2 (Build 25C56)

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (e076690)

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

_No response_

author: slferris

title: jj prompt override doesn't work when using git async-prompt
body:
### Describe the bug

The Oh-My-Zsh [jj plugin installation doc](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/tree/master/plugins/jj) recommends this override to add a jj status to the prompt:

```
_my_theme_vcs_info() {
  jj_prompt_template 'self.change_id().shortest(3)' \
  || git_prompt_info
}

PROMPT='$(_my_theme_vcs_info) $'
```

However, this override breaks when git async prompt is enabled, which is the default (that is, `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt` is explicitly enabled or empty).

In that case, `git_prompt_info` just stops working, and returns nothing.

## Note

This is also the case when simply adding an extra level of indirection around `git_prompt_info` :

```
_my_git_prompt_info() {
  git_prompt_info
}

PROMPT='$(_my_git_prompt_info) $'
```

In this case, when async-prompt is enabled, `git_prompt_info` stops working, and returns nothing.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Edit the robbyrussel theme,
2. Replace `PROMPT+=' $(git_prompt_info)'` by:
    ```
    _my_git_prompt_info() {
      git_prompt_info
    }
    PROMPT+=' $(_my_git_prompt_info)'
    ```
3. Notice how the git info is no longer displayed in a git directory.

### Expected behavior

1. The `git_prompt_info` should work even in a wrapper function,
2. OR the jj-plugin installation doc should mention the need to disable git async-mode.

### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

macOS 15.7.3

### Zsh version

5.9

### Oh My Zsh version

master (9df4ea0)

### Terminal emulator

iTerm2

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

None

### Additional context

## Workaround

Manually disabling the git async prompt makes `git_prompt_info` work again – even in a wrapper function:

```
zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt false
```

author: NN---

title: Introduce versioning
body:
This issue supersedes:

- #9216 
- #10468

----

Steps:

- [x] Define versioning and release model
- [x] Add branches and configure GitHub
- [ ] #13650
- [ ] Support releases in CLI
- [ ] Support releases in installer
- [ ] Research or add automations for GitHub Actions
- [ ] Define support model (which releases we keep supporting)

----

## Versioning model

We have chosen [Calendar Versioning](https://calver.org/), while keeping in line with semantic versioning.

We will have the following version schema: `YY0M.MINOR.PATCH`

- `YY0M` (e.g. 2603): they will act as "major" in SemVer, i.e. a change will signify there are breaking changes.
- MINOR: new features
- PATCH: bug and security fixes

On a new "major" release:

- We will create a new branch
- We will tag the release with `YY0M.0.0`

On a new Minor or Patch release:

- We will cherry-pick the commits onto the "major" branch
- We will tag the release on the HEAD of the branch

## Ideas

- Using `git config` as a backend for storing the versioning. Currently in use:
  - `oh-my-zsh.branch` (branch name)
  - `oh-my-zsh.lastVersion` (commit head)
- Release branches: `release/YY0M` (e.g. release/2603)
- Release tags: `vYY0M.X.X`
- Protect release branches in GitHub (by prefix)

## Automations

> These are ideas for the future, we'll work manually for now.

- A GitHub Action to backport a PR on a release branch
- Create new releases on GitHub when they happen, adding the changelog

## CLI

- `omz update`: support release model, branches and tags
- `omz update`: notify the user when they're on a non-release branch or commit
- `omz version`: notify the user when there's a new branch

title: Add sub-commands to the SSH plugin to support port forwarding and proxy.
body:
### If the feature request is for a plugin or theme, specify it here.

ssh

### If the feature solves a problem you have, specify it here.

I added 2 functionalities to ssh plugin that allow you to easily create 2 types of ssh proxy.

### Describe the proposed feature.

new functions:
`ssh port-forward` -> lets you bind a local port to a specific server port
`ssh proxy` -> lets you to create a local socks5 proxy that forwards any traffic sent to it to the remote host.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

Both `ssh port-forward` and `ssh proxy` have a shorter sub-command, making them even easier to use.
`ssh pf` -> `ssh port-forward`
`ssh px` -> 'ssh proxy'

### Reasons
1. Port forwarding simplifies the process for developers or others to connect to local services hosted on a remote server from their local machine. This is particularly useful when running databases or other services on the server.

2. Allowing the forwarding of traffic from your local machine to a server makes troubleshooting network issues on the server easier.

author: o-l-a-v

title: bug[#13615]: [NIT]tools/update.sh -- fix minor redundant variable declaration
body: # NIT: tools/update.sh -- fix minor redundant variable declaration PR: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/13615 ## Summary 1. `tools/upgrade.sh` contained two incorrect uses of the `local` builtin at script (top-level) scope. The `local` keyword is only valid inside a shell function. Using it at the top level of a shell script is an error. 2. `tools/upgrade.sh` contained two declarations for the `ret` variable. ## Background ### How it was discovered Running the script accidentally with `sh` instead of `zsh`. `sh tools/upgrade.sh` Produced an error such as: > tools/upgrade.sh: line 4: local: can only be used in a function ### Why zsh silently accepted it zsh treats local at script scope as a non-fatal extension — it simply performs a normal variable assignment. This is a known zsh behavior, but it is considered incorrect shell scripting. The result was that the bug had no visible impact under normal zsh usage, so it went undetected. ### The two issues found 1. Line 4 — `local ret=0 # exit code` -- ret is used throughout the script to track the exit code. Declaring it with local at the top level is invalid; it should just be `ret=0`. 2. Line 228 — `local ret=0` -- A second, bare declaration of ret midway through the script. This is dead code: ret is already in scope from line 4, and by line 228, it has been actively used. This re-declaration had no effect and appears to be a copy-paste artifact. ## Fix Both issues were addressed in PR #13615: - local ret=0 → ret=0 at line 4 - Removed the duplicate local ret=0 at line 228 ## Lesson Scripts intended to run only under zsh can still contain POSIX compliance errors that go undetected because of zsh's permissive extension behavior. Running or linting scripts with a strict POSIX shell (e.g., sh, dash) can surface these hidden issues. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Navigate to your oh-my-zsh directory (e.g. ~/.oh-my-zsh). 2. Run the upgrade script using sh instead of zsh: `sh tools/upgrade.sh` 3. Observe the error printed to the terminal before the script exits. ### Expected behavior Running sh tools/upgrade.sh should produce no warning. The script completes without any output related to local or variable declarations. ### Screenshots and recordings ### OS / Linux distribution macOS 26.3.1 (25D2128) ### Zsh version 5.9 ### Oh My Zsh version zsh 5.9 (arm-apple-darwin25.0.0) ### Terminal emulator iTerm2 ### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL None ### Additional context _No response_

author: 2eg01nd

title: Support releases in updater and changelog
body:
By default, users are on the default branch (configured via git config).

There should be a setting to pick either:

1. **Default branch**: usual approach.
2. **A stable major release**: updates to all minor and patch updates).
3. **A specific stable release version**: no automatic changes, there will only be a forced update to the latest version on `YY0M` if there is a security patch. For example: if on `2603.1.1`, it will be updated to `2603.9.1` if there was a vulnerability.

The changelog should support showing changes specific between last and new version (I think it already works for this).
author: mcornella

title: Suggestion: More prominent post-install reminder to merge old .zshrc customizations
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem?

Hi there! First of all, thanks for the awesome project — I use Oh My Zsh every day and love it. 🙌

I just ran into a small hiccup after a fresh install: the installer replaced my `~/.zshrc` with the default template (and kindly backed up the old one to `~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh`). However, I didn't notice the backup message in the terminal output, so I spent a while wondering why several CLI tools (that relied on PATH entries and shell functions defined in my old `.zshrc`) suddenly stopped working.

I imagine this is a fairly common experience for new users who have accumulated custom configurations — conda init blocks, proxy settings, PATH exports, shell functions, etc.

### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be really helpful if the installer printed a **more prominent reminder** after installation, something like:

```
⚠️  Your previous ~/.zshrc has been backed up to ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
    If you had custom configurations (PATH exports, aliases, functions, etc.),
    you may want to migrate them to your new ~/.zshrc.

    You can compare the two files with:
      diff ~/.zshrc ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
```

This could be a simple addition to the post-install output — no changes to the actual install logic needed.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Automatically appending old custom configurations to the new `.zshrc` — but this could introduce conflicts, so I understand why it's not done.
- Adding a note in the README — but most users follow quick-start instructions and may not read the full docs.

### Additional context

The backup mechanism itself is great! This is purely about making the "next step" more visible so users don't get stuck. Thanks for considering this! 😊
author: ShiLong-CN

title: Suggestion: More prominent post-install reminder to merge old .zshrc customizations
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem?

Hi there! First of all, thanks for the awesome project — I use Oh My Zsh every day and love it. 🙌

I just ran into a small hiccup after a fresh install: the installer replaced my `~/.zshrc` with the default template (and kindly backed up the old one to `~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh`). However, I didn't notice the backup message in the terminal output, so I spent a while wondering why several CLI tools (that relied on PATH entries and shell functions defined in my old `.zshrc`) suddenly stopped working.

I imagine this is a fairly common experience for new users who have accumulated custom configurations — conda init blocks, proxy settings, PATH exports, shell functions, etc.

### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be really helpful if the installer printed a **more prominent reminder** after installation, something like:

```
⚠️  Your previous ~/.zshrc has been backed up to ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
    If you had custom configurations (PATH exports, aliases, functions, etc.),
    you may want to migrate them to your new ~/.zshrc.

    You can compare the two files with:
      diff ~/.zshrc ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
```

This could be a simple addition to the post-install output — no changes to the actual install logic needed.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Automatically appending old custom configurations to the new `.zshrc` — but this could introduce conflicts, so I understand why it's not done.
- Adding a note in the README — but most users follow quick-start instructions and may not read the full docs.

### Additional context

The backup mechanism itself is great! This is purely about making the "next step" more visible so users don't get stuck. Thanks for considering this! 😊

author: benrfairless

title: git prompt shows extra %% after branch name when histsubstpattern is enabled
body:
## Bug Report                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  In `lib/git.zsh` line 39, the parameter expansion `${ref:gs/%/%%}` produces incorrect output when the `histsubstpattern` zsh option is enabled.                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  ### Problem                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  With `histsubstpattern` set (which oh-my-zsh enables), the `%` in `:gs/%/%%` is interpreted as a **pattern matching the end of the string** instead of a literal `%`. This causes `%%` to be appended to the branch name even when it contains no `%`.                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Example:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
  - Branch name: `main`                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  - Expected output: `main`                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  - Actual output: `main%%`                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  This results in the prompt displaying `git:(main%%)` instead of `git:(main)`.                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  ### Reproduction                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  ```zsh                                                    
  setopt histsubstpattern                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  ref=main                                                  
  echo "${ref:gs/%/%%}"   # outputs: main%%                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Cause                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  The :gs flag uses pattern matching when histsubstpattern is enabled. % is a zsh pattern that matches the end of the string.                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Fix                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
                                                            
  Replace :gs/%/%% with the standard parameter expansion syntax //\%/%%:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
                                                            
  -  echo "${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX}${ref:gs/%/%%}${upstream:gs/%/%%}$(parse_git_dirty)${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX}"                                                                                                                                                                                 
  +  echo "${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX}${ref//\%/%%}${upstream//\%/%%}$(parse_git_dirty)${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX}"                                                                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Environment                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  - oh-my-zsh: latest (commit 349b9e4)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - zsh: 5.9
  - OS: macOS Darwin 25.2.0
author: lanshankj

title: `lib/termsupport.zsh` appears to trigger Windows Terminal freeze on tab close under Arch Linux on WSL 2
body:
### Describe the bug

On Arch Linux running under WSL 2, closing a Windows Terminal tab that is running `zsh` with Oh My Zsh frequently causes Windows Terminal to freeze temporarily.

After a fairly long narrowing-down process, the issue appears to be strongly correlated with `oh-my-zsh` terminal integration from `lib/termsupport.zsh`, especially the `omz_termsupport_cwd` hook.

Disabling only `omz_termsupport_cwd` significantly improves the problem on my system, while keeping most of Oh My Zsh enabled.

This does **not** seem to be a general `zsh` exit problem:
- launching `zsh -f` makes the issue much less likely
- replacing `powerlevel10k` with `starship` did **not** remove the issue
- no custom `zlogout` or similar logout logic was involved

### Steps to reproduce

My exact config is customized, so I cannot yet provide a clean minimal reproduction, but the behavior is:

1. Run Arch Linux under WSL 2 in Windows Terminal
2. Start an interactive `zsh` session using Oh My Zsh
3. Close the Windows Terminal tab
4. Windows Terminal frequently freezes for a short period instead of closing cleanly

This happened both when:
- launching the Arch distro directly in Windows Terminal
- launching via `wsl -d archlinux` from PowerShell

### Expected behavior

Closing the tab should shut down the interactive shell promptly and Windows Terminal should close smoothly.


### Screenshots and recordings

_No response_

### OS / Linux distribution

Arch Linux (rolling) under WSL 2

### Zsh version

5.9

### Terminal emulator

Windows Terminal

### If using WSL on Windows, which version of WSL

WSL2

### Additional context

### What I found

I instrumented `zsh` exit to log hook state, signals, and file descriptors during teardown.

The abnormal path looked like this:

- the shell lost its PTY first (`/dev/pts/N` became deleted)
- the shell then received `SIGHUP`
- shell teardown continued after the terminal was already gone
- terminal-facing Oh My Zsh hooks were still loaded at that point

I did **not** find evidence of:
- stuck user jobs
- `gitstatusd`
- a custom logout script
- Starship itself being the cause

The remaining suspect set was the terminal-facing hooks from `lib/termsupport.zsh`:
- `omz_termsupport_precmd`
- `omz_termsupport_preexec`
- `omz_termsupport_cwd`

The most effective targeted mitigation so far has been:

```zsh
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
add-zsh-hook -d precmd omz_termsupport_cwd 2>/dev/null || true
```

That disables only the OSC 7 cwd reporting hook while leaving title updates enabled.

### Why I think `termsupport` is involved

The issue became much less frequent when I disabled the `termsupport` hooks entirely.

Then I restored the two title-related hooks:
- `omz_termsupport_precmd`
- `omz_termsupport_preexec`

and kept only `omz_termsupport_cwd` disabled.

With only `omz_termsupport_cwd` removed, the system became reasonably stable again.

That suggests the most suspicious path is the cwd-reporting OSC sequence emitted by `omz_termsupport_cwd`, at least on this Arch + WSL 2 + Windows Terminal combination.

### Current workaround

After sourcing Oh My Zsh:

```zsh
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
add-zsh-hook -d precmd omz_termsupport_cwd 2>/dev/null || true
```

### Current system information

- OS: Arch Linux (rolling) under WSL 2
- Kernel: `Linux Konjac-XZ-PC 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Jun 5 18:30:46 UTC 2025 x86_64 GNU/Linux`
- `/etc/os-release`:
  - `PRETTY_NAME="Arch Linux"`
  - `ID=arch`
- `zsh --version`: `zsh 5.9 (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)`
- virtualization: `wsl`
- `WSL_DISTRO_NAME=archlinux`
- `TERM=xterm-256color`
- `TERM_PROGRAM=` (empty)
- Oh My Zsh commit: `7c10d9839f05b8b73e26aa4e0f04cc886fbba6a6`

### Active hook state in the current "stable" workaround configuration

`precmd_functions`:
- `_direnv_hook`
- `_defer_async_git_register`
- `_omz_async_request`
- `omz_termsupport_precmd`
- `_zsh_autosuggest_start`
- `_zsh_highlight_main__precmd_hook`
- `prompt_starship_precmd`

`preexec_functions`:
- `omz_termsupport_preexec`
- `_zsh_highlight_preexec_hook`
- `prompt_starship_preexec`

`chpwd_functions`:
- `_direnv_hook`
- `__zoxide_hook`

In other words, `omz_termsupport_cwd` is the only `termsupport` hook currently removed.

### Additional note

I also tested with a different prompt stack:
- previously `powerlevel10k`
- now `starship`

The freeze still occurred until I started restricting/disabling `termsupport`, so this does not appear to be specific to the prompt theme.

If helpful, I can provide my exit instrumentation logs, but the main relevant observation is that on the bad path the shell is already detached from the PTY before teardown completes.

author: adambullmer

title: oh-my-zsh.sh compinit cache is invalidated on every startup when $fpath contains entries from other shell init files (brew, pyenv, conda, etc.)
body:
### Problem

Oh-My-Zsh's compinit caching logic in `oh-my-zsh.sh` (approx. lines 113–132) compares the current `$fpath` against a stored metadata line in `$ZSH_COMPDUMP` using `grep -Fx` (exact whole-line match). In many common setups, the `$fpath` string differs subtly between sessions, causing OMZ to **delete the dump and do a full compinit rebuild on every shell startup**.

The underlying `.zcompdump` file is correct — OMZ just never trusts it, wasting 500–1500 ms per new shell.

### How to reproduce

Minimal reproduction on a fresh OMZ install:

1. Add a line to `~/.zprofile` that modifies `$fpath`. Any of the following will do:
   ```sh
   eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"     # macOS Homebrew
   # or
   eval "$(pyenv init -)"
   # or
   eval "$(conda shell.zsh hook)"
   ```

2. Measure two consecutive shell startups:
   ```sh
   time zsh -i -c exit     # First run
   time zsh -i -c exit     # Second run
   ```

3. Confirm the dump is being rewritten each time:
   ```sh
   stat -f "%Sm" ~/.zcompdump-*    # macOS
   # or: stat -c "%y" ~/.zcompdump-*    # Linux
   zsh -i -c exit
   stat -f "%Sm" ~/.zcompdump-*    # timestamp changed
   ```

### Correct behavior

After the first shell startup builds the dump, subsequent startups should reuse it (via `compinit -C`) until either:
- The dump is older than a configured TTL, OR
- The set of completion sources has actually changed

Second-run startup time should be dramatically faster than the first run.

### Actual behavior

Second-run startup time is identical to the first run. The `.zcompdump` file is deleted and rebuilt every time, because OMZ's fpath metadata check fails.

On a representative OMZ install with ~13 plugins (git, brew, fzf, kubectl, npm, etc.):

| Configuration | Startup | compinit | compdef calls |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current OMZ (cache always invalidated) | ~1100–1300 ms | ~1100 ms | ~820 |
| With `compinit -C` (cache trusted) | ~130 ms | ~15 ms | ~17 |

### Root cause

Current OMZ logic on `master`:

```zsh
# Construct zcompdump OMZ metadata
zcompdump_revision="#omz revision: $(builtin cd -q "$ZSH"; git rev-parse HEAD 2>/dev/null)"
zcompdump_fpath="#omz fpath: $fpath"

# Delete the zcompdump file if OMZ zcompdump metadata changed
if ! command grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_revision" "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" 2>/dev/null \
   || ! command grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_fpath" "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" 2>/dev/null; then
  command rm -f "$ZSH_COMPDUMP"
  zcompdump_refresh=1
fi
```

The fpath check uses `grep -Fx` against `$fpath` joined as a single string. This is brittle because:

1. **Order matters.** `a b c` does not match `a c b`. Tools that prepend vs. append to `fpath` can produce different orderings between sessions.
2. **Duplicates matter.** If a path appears twice in one session and three times in another, the strings don't match even though the effective fpath is identical.
3. **`fpath` is mutable before OMZ runs.** `.zprofile`, `.zshenv`, Apple's `/etc/zshenv`, `brew shellenv`, pyenv/conda/nvm/mise hooks all inject entries with variable timing.

Example of the observed drift between sessions on a Homebrew setup:

```
Session N stored: #omz fpath: .../site-functions .../zsh-completions .../plugins/git ...
Session N+1 live: #omz fpath: .../site-functions .../site-functions .../zsh-completions ...
                               ^^^ duplicate injected by brew shellenv + brew plugin
```

The `grep -Fx` fails, dump is deleted, full rebuild runs, dump is rewritten with session N+1's fpath, and the cycle repeats next session.

### Environment

Please replace with your actual values when filing:

```
OMZ version:    061f773dd356df52a8bccd5e73377c012f97ef14 (2026-04-13)
Zsh version:    zsh 5.9 (arm64-apple-darwin25.0)
OS / kernel:    Darwin 25.4.0 Darwin Kernel Version 25.4.0 arm64 (macOS, Apple Silicon)
Terminal:       Warp v0.2026.04.22
Relevant tools: Homebrew 5.1.7, pyenv 2.6.1, conda 26.3.2, mise 2025.4.0, direnv 2.35.0
```

### Self-contained reproduction

Attached as a gist: [`reproduce.sh`](https://gist.github.com/rndjams/c7a3e85348ecb348f2d937c81628e49a) — a ~160-line zsh script
that isolates the bug in a `mktemp -d` sandbox (does not touch your
real `$HOME`). It:

1. Clones a fresh OMZ into `$TMPDIR`
2. Writes a minimal `.zshrc` (only the `git` plugin) and a `.zprofile`
   that perturbs `$fpath` between sessions
3. Runs `zsh -i -c exit` several times
4. Reports whether the dump was rewritten between runs

Expected behavior on current master: dump rewritten on every run where
fpath differs from the stored metadata (several distinct mtimes across
runs). Expected behavior with the proposed fix: one mtime across all
runs.

Sample output included in the gist as `SAMPLE-OUTPUT.md`, showing both
the bug on master and the expected result after the fix.

### Related issues and PRs

Searched before filing (per CONTRIBUTING guidelines):

- #8536 — "Why my Oh My Zsh startup is slow" (Jan 2020, open, `Performance` label). Symptoms match this root cause but the issue never identified the fpath-string comparison as the culprit.
- #7642 — "After enabling a plugin, autocompletion requires manual rm of a `.zcompdump` file" (2019, open). Related but the *opposite* problem (cache not invalidated when it should be).
- [forivall:oh-my-zsh @ `use-cached-compdump`](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/compare/master...forivall:oh-my-zsh:use-cached-compdump) — Nov 2024 branch by @forivall adding an opt-in `ZSH_COMPINIT_CACHE=true` flag. Has not been filed as a PR as of April 2026.
- [ctechols gist](https://gist.github.com/ctechols/ca1035271ad134841284) — 213-star community workaround from 2015. The canonical "`compinit -C` if dump is fresh" pattern.
- [mattmc3/ez-compinit](https://github.com/mattmc3/ez-compinit) — alternative plugin that replaces OMZ's compinit handling entirely.

### Proposed solutions (for discussion)

Three options, increasing in invasiveness:

**Option A — Normalize fpath before comparison.** Sort and dedupe the fpath string so ordering and duplicates don't invalidate the cache:
```zsh
zcompdump_fpath="#omz fpath: ${${(@onu)fpath}}"
```
Preserves current semantics ("invalidate when fpath content changes"), fixes the common case silently. Existing dumps invalidated once on upgrade.

Note on syntax: the nested `${...}` is required. Single-form `${(onu)fpath}` silently does not sort/dedupe in a string context — the flags need array-element expansion via `@`, then re-wrapping for interpolation.

**Option B — Opt-in time-based cache (forivall's approach).** Add `ZSH_COMPINIT_CACHE=true` that short-circuits to `compinit -C -d "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" -D` when the dump is less than 24 h old. Zero impact on existing users.

**Option C — Combine A and B.** Fix the common case for all users (A), and offer power users the fastest path (B).

### Would a PR be welcome?

If so, which option (A/B/C or something else) would be preferred? I'm happy to open a PR once maintainers indicate a direction. Drafting is already in progress, with credit to @forivall for the prior-art branch on Option B.

cc @forivall (prior work on `use-cached-compdump` branch)

---

**AI-assisted disclosure (per CONTRIBUTING.md):** This issue was drafted with AI assistance during root-cause investigation on a real Homebrew+OMZ setup. All code references and measurements were verified locally. The author understands the fix and can explain/defend each part on request.
